projects.name: Dangote Petroleum Refinery
projects.tagline: Africa's largest oil refinery — reshaping energy independence on the continent.
projects.overview: The Dangote Petroleum Refinery is a 650,000 barrels-per-day integrated refinery and petrochemical complex located in the Lekki Free Trade Zone near Lagos, Nigeria. Developed by Dangote Industries Limited, it is the largest single-train refinery in the world and the largest in Africa. The project aims to transform Nigeria from a net importer of refined petroleum products to a self-sufficient producer and potential exporter, fundamentally changing the energy economics of West Africa. The refinery sits on 2,635 hectares of land with direct deep-sea port access, and includes a 435 MW captive power plant capable of powering a small city.
objectives: Achieve energy self-sufficiency for Nigeria by refining crude oil domestically instead of exporting raw crude and importing refined products
Eliminate Nigeria's dependence on imported refined petroleum products, which previously cost the country over $10 billion annually in foreign exchange
Create over 100,000 direct and indirect jobs across the entire value chain from construction through full operations
Produce Euro-V quality fuels that meet international emission standards for cleaner air in African cities
Supply petrochemical feedstock including polypropylene to local manufacturing industries, reducing import dependency for plastics and packaging
Position Nigeria and West Africa as a net exporter of refined petroleum products for the first time in history
technologies: Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) unit for high-yield gasoline production from heavy crude fractions
Hydrocracking units for producing ultra-low-sulfur diesel and jet fuel meeting Euro-V emission standards
Polypropylene production plant with 900,000 tonnes per year capacity — one of the largest in the world
1,100 km subsea pipeline infrastructure for crude oil delivery directly from offshore production platforms
Advanced flue gas desulfurization and environmental monitoring systems for emissions control
435 MW captive power plant using combined-cycle gas turbine technology for self-sufficient energy supply

milestones.title: Project Announcement & Ground Breaking
milestones.description: Dangote Group announces the refinery project with an initial investment estimate of $9 billion. Ground-breaking ceremony held at the Lekki Free Trade Zone site with support from the Nigerian federal government.

milestones.title: Major Construction Phase Begins
milestones.description: Full-scale construction commences following completion of site preparation. Over 30,000 workers deployed on-site. Key process units including the crude distillation unit and residual fluid catalytic cracking unit begin fabrication.

milestones.title: Mechanical Completion of Core Units
milestones.description: The crude distillation unit and several secondary processing units reach mechanical completion despite COVID-19 delays. Pre-commissioning testing and calibration of safety systems begins across the facility.

milestones.title: First Crude Oil Received & Diesel Production
milestones.description: The refinery receives its first cargo of crude oil — 1 million barrels — and begins initial processing operations. Diesel (AGO) and aviation fuel (Jet-A1) production commences. First truck-out of diesel delivered to the Nigerian domestic market in January 2024.

milestones.title: Petrol (PMS) Production Begins
milestones.description: The fluid catalytic cracking unit comes online, enabling production of Premium Motor Spirit (petrol/gasoline). First petrol delivery to the Nigerian National Petroleum Company (NNPC) in September 2024, marking a historic milestone in Nigeria's downstream sector.

milestones.title: Full Capacity Ramp-Up & Petrochemicals
milestones.description: Progressive ramp-up toward full 650,000 bpd processing capacity with all secondary units operational. Polypropylene production begins for domestic and export markets. Refinery targets processing multiple crude grades including Nigerian Bonny Light, Forcados, and Escravos blends.

resources.name: Crude Oil Feedstock
resources.detail: 650,000 barrels per day capacity, primarily Nigerian crude grades (Bonny Light, Forcados, Qua Iboe). Can also process other African and international crude blends.

resources.name: Natural Gas Supply
resources.detail: Gas supply for hydrogen production, power generation via the 435 MW plant, and process heating. Sourced through dedicated pipeline infrastructure.

resources.name: Water (Desalination)
resources.detail: On-site seawater desalination plant producing process water and cooling water, reducing dependence on municipal freshwater supply.

resources.name: Land & Port Infrastructure
resources.detail: 2,635 hectares in the Lekki Free Trade Zone with a dedicated deep-sea port capable of receiving Very Large Crude Carriers (VLCCs) up to 300,000 DWT.

resources.name: Workforce & Skills Transfer
resources.detail: Peak construction workforce of 30,000+. Over 100,000 direct and indirect jobs in operations. Extensive skills transfer and technical training programs for Nigerian engineers and operators.

projects.impact: The refinery is projected to save Nigeria over $10 billion annually in foreign exchange that was previously spent importing refined fuel products. It has created tens of thousands of skilled jobs and is catalyzing downstream industries in plastics, manufacturing, packaging, and logistics across West Africa. By producing Euro-V quality fuels domestically, it also reduces the environmental impact of lower-grade imported fuels that previously dominated the Nigerian market.
projects.videoTitle: Inside Africa's Largest Refinery — The Dangote Story

projects.name: Tulu Kapi Gold Project
projects.tagline: Ethiopia's first large-scale commercial gold mine — unlocking the country's mineral potential.
projects.region: Helu Abayi Woreda, Western Wellega Zone, Oromia
projects.overview: The Tulu Kapi Gold Project is a major open-pit and underground gold mining development located in the western Wellega Zone of the Oromia region in Ethiopia, approximately 520 km west of Addis Ababa. Developed by KEFI Gold and Copper plc (listed on AIM London), it is Ethiopia's first large-scale commercial gold mine and represents one of the most significant foreign direct investments in the country's mining sector. The project holds a JORC-compliant mineral resource of approximately 1.7 million ounces of gold, with an initial open-pit mine life of over 8 years and substantial underground expansion potential. Tulu Kapi is positioned to establish Ethiopia as a credible destination for international mining investment in East Africa.
objectives: Establish Ethiopia's first internationally-financed, large-scale commercial gold mine to unlock the country's mineral wealth
Produce approximately 140,000 ounces of gold per year during the initial open-pit phase at competitive all-in sustaining costs
Create over 1,500 direct jobs during operations and up to 4,000 jobs during construction, with priority given to local communities
Demonstrate responsible mining practices with comprehensive environmental and social management plans approved by Ethiopian authorities
Generate significant tax revenue and royalties for the Ethiopian federal and regional governments
Build local infrastructure including roads, water supply, and power systems that benefit surrounding communities beyond the mine's life
technologies: Conventional open-pit mining using drill-and-blast with hydraulic excavators and rigid dump trucks
Carbon-in-leach (CIL) gold processing plant with 1.8 million tonnes per annum throughput capacity
Gravity separation circuit for coarse gold recovery prior to leaching, improving overall extraction rates
Tailings storage facility (TSF) designed to international standards with downstream dam construction method
On-site assay laboratory and geological core-logging facility for ongoing resource definition and grade control
Grid power connection from Ethiopian national electricity network (hydroelectric), reducing reliance on diesel generators

milestones.title: KEFI Acquires Tulu Kapi License
milestones.description: KEFI Minerals (now KEFI Gold and Copper) acquires the Tulu Kapi gold exploration and mining license in the Oromia region of western Ethiopia, following positive exploration results showing significant gold mineralization.

milestones.title: Definitive Feasibility Study Completed
milestones.description: The Definitive Feasibility Study (DFS) confirms Tulu Kapi as a technically and economically robust gold project. The study outlines an 8+ year open-pit mine producing ~140,000 oz/year gold with an initial CAPEX of approximately $265 million.

milestones.title: Mining License Granted by Ethiopian Government
milestones.description: The Ethiopian Ministry of Mines and Petroleum grants the large-scale mining license for Tulu Kapi, a landmark approval that signals Ethiopia's commitment to developing its mining sector with international partners.

milestones.title: Community Resettlement Program Begins
milestones.description: The Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) is approved and implementation begins. Over 1,200 households within the mine footprint are to be relocated to a purpose-built resettlement village with improved housing, water supply, schools, and health facilities.

milestones.title: Project Restructured as Government JV
milestones.description: The project is restructured as a joint venture between KEFI (80% economic interest) and the Ethiopian government. The updated feasibility study shows improved economics with gold price environment. Financing agreements advanced with international lenders.

milestones.title: Construction & Development Phase
milestones.description: Early works and site preparation underway. Full construction mobilization targeting processing plant build-out, tailings facility, and mine infrastructure. First gold pour anticipated approximately 24 months after full construction commencement.

resources.name: Gold Ore Body
resources.detail: JORC-compliant resource of approximately 1.7 million ounces of gold. Open-pit reserve of ~1.0 Moz at 2.1 g/t gold, with additional underground potential at higher grades.

resources.name: Hydroelectric Power
resources.detail: Grid connection to Ethiopia's national electricity network, predominantly supplied by hydroelectric dams (Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam and others), providing clean, low-cost energy.

resources.name: Water Supply
resources.detail: Water sourced from local rivers with a comprehensive water management plan including recycling within the processing circuit and monitoring of downstream water quality.

resources.name: Mining License Area
resources.detail: Large-scale mining license covering the Tulu Kapi deposit area in Helu Abayi Woreda, with road access via upgraded routes from the regional capital Nekemte.

resources.name: Local Workforce
resources.detail: Up to 4,000 jobs during construction and 1,500+ during operations. Priority hiring from local Oromia communities with training and skills development programs.

projects.impact: Tulu Kapi is projected to generate over $400 million in tax revenue and royalties for the Ethiopian government over the life of the mine. It will create thousands of jobs in a region with limited formal employment opportunities, and the resettlement program is providing improved housing, schools, health clinics, and water supply to displaced communities. The project is also catalyzing road and infrastructure improvements in western Oromia that benefit the broader region beyond mining activity. As Ethiopia's first large-scale commercial gold mine, Tulu Kapi is a proof-of-concept for the country's mining sector, potentially unlocking billions of dollars in future mineral investment across East Africa.

projects.name: Kamoa-Kakula Copper Mine
projects.tagline: The world's fastest-growing major copper mine, reshaping the DRC's mining landscape.
projects.overview: Kamoa-Kakula is a tier-one copper mining complex discovered in 2006 and brought into production in 2021. It is jointly owned by Ivanhoe Mines (39.6%), Zijin Mining (39.6%), Crystal River Global (0.8%), and the DRC government (20%). In 2023, the mine produced approximately 394,000 tonnes of copper in concentrate, making it one of the largest copper producers globally. Phase 3 expansion with a 500 ktpa smelter is underway to produce blister copper domestically.
objectives: Achieve annualized copper production exceeding 600,000 tonnes per year by 2026
Commission the on-site direct-to-blister flash smelter to process concentrate domestically
Develop the Kamoa-Kakula underground mining complex across multiple phases
Generate sustainable economic benefits and employment for the Lualaba Province
Establish industry-leading environmental and safety standards for underground mining
Contribute to global copper supply for the clean energy transition
technologies: Underground drift-and-fill mining method
Direct-to-blister flash copper smelter
Controlled convergence room-and-pillar mining
Dense media separation pre-concentration
Flotation circuit for copper sulphide concentration
Paste tailings backfill system

milestones.title: Discovery
milestones.description: Robert Friedland and Ivanhoe Mines discovered the Kamoa copper deposit in Lualaba Province.

milestones.title: Kakula Discovery
milestones.description: The ultra-high-grade Kakula deposit was discovered adjacent to Kamoa, significantly expanding the resource.

milestones.title: Phase 1 Production
milestones.description: First copper concentrate production began in May 2021, seven months ahead of schedule.

milestones.title: Phase 2 Commissioning
milestones.description: Phase 2 concentrator plant was commissioned, doubling processing capacity to 7.6 Mtpa.

milestones.title: Phase 3 Smelter Construction
milestones.description: Construction of the 500,000-tonne-per-year direct-to-blister flash smelter advanced significantly.

milestones.title: Smelter Commissioning
milestones.description: The on-site smelter is expected to begin commissioning, enabling domestic copper processing.

resources.name: Copper Ore Reserve
resources.detail: The Kamoa-Kakula complex hosts an indicated mineral resource of approximately 1.04 billion tonnes grading 2.69% copper.

resources.name: Phase 1 & 2 Concentrators
resources.detail: Combined processing capacity of 7.6 million tonnes per annum of ore through two flotation concentrator plants.

resources.name: Direct-to-Blister Smelter
resources.detail: A 500,000 tpa flash smelter designed to produce over 99% pure blister copper on-site.

resources.name: Underground Mine Infrastructure
resources.detail: Multiple underground mine access declines and ventilation shafts serving the Kansoko, Kakula, and Kamoa North mining areas.

resources.name: Turbine Power Supply
resources.detail: Dedicated 220 kV power line from the Inga and Mwadingusha hydroelectric stations providing clean energy to the mine.

projects.impact: Kamoa-Kakula has created over 20,000 direct and indirect jobs in one of the DRC's least-developed provinces. The mine contributed over $500 million in taxes, royalties, and fees to the DRC government in 2023 alone. It has transformed the town of Kolwezi into a major mining hub and is positioning the DRC as the world's second-largest copper producer.

projects.name: Kibali Gold Mine
projects.tagline: Africa's largest gold mine, producing over 700,000 ounces annually from the heart of the DRC.
projects.overview: Kibali is one of the world's largest gold mines, situated in the remote northeast of the DRC. It is operated by Barrick Gold (45%) in a joint venture with AngloGold Ashanti (45%) and the DRC state mining company SOKIMO (10%). The mine poured its first gold in September 2013 and produced approximately 750,000 ounces in 2023. Kibali combines open-pit and underground operations with a state-of-the-art metallurgical complex.
objectives: Sustain gold production above 700,000 ounces per year through underground expansion
Extend mine life through continued exploration and resource conversion
Operate three hydroelectric power stations to minimize carbon footprint
Deliver economic development to one of the DRC's most remote regions
Maintain industry-leading safety and environmental management standards
Transition progressively from open-pit to underground mining
technologies: Sublevel caving underground mining method
Automated underground haulage with autonomous trucks
Carbon-in-leach (CIL) gold processing circuit
Gravity separation gold recovery
Three on-site hydroelectric power stations (Nzoro, Ambarau, Azambi)
Paste backfill plant for underground void management

milestones.title: Joint Venture Formed
milestones.description: Barrick Gold and AngloGold Ashanti formed a joint venture to develop the Kibali gold deposit.

milestones.title: First Gold Pour
milestones.description: Kibali poured its first gold bar in September 2013, marking the start of commercial production.

milestones.title: Underground Production Begins
milestones.description: Underground mining commenced, adding higher-grade ore to supplement the open pit.

milestones.title: Record Production
milestones.description: The mine achieved record annual gold production of over 814,000 ounces.

milestones.title: Autonomous Haulage
milestones.description: Kibali deployed automated underground haulage trucks, becoming one of the first in Africa to do so.

milestones.title: Continued Underground Expansion
milestones.description: Ongoing development of deeper underground levels to sustain production through the 2030s.

resources.name: Gold Mineral Reserve
resources.detail: Proven and probable reserves of approximately 11.3 million ounces of gold as of year-end 2023.

resources.name: Metallurgical Complex
resources.detail: A 7.2 Mtpa processing plant with gravity separation, flotation, CIL, and ultra-fine grind circuits.

resources.name: Hydroelectric Power
resources.detail: Three run-of-river hydroelectric stations on the Kibali River providing approximately 44 MW of clean power.

resources.name: Underground Mine
resources.detail: Sublevel caving operation accessed via twin declines, producing the majority of ore feed.

resources.name: Exploration Tenements
resources.detail: Extensive exploration permits covering over 3,000 square kilometres surrounding the mine.

projects.impact: Kibali employs over 5,500 people directly and supports approximately 11,000 indirect jobs, with over 95% of the workforce being Congolese nationals. The mine has invested more than $250 million in community development since inception, including schools, health clinics, roads, and agricultural programs. It generates substantial tax revenues and royalties for both the national and provincial governments.

projects.name: Obuasi Gold Mine
projects.tagline: A century-old Ghanaian gold mine reborn through a $545 million modern redevelopment.
projects.overview: Obuasi is one of the world's premier gold ore bodies, having produced over 30 million ounces since mining began in 1897. Owned by AngloGold Ashanti, the mine was placed on care and maintenance in 2016 and subsequently redeveloped into a modern mechanized underground operation. The Phase 3 redevelopment, costing approximately $545 million, brought the mine back to commercial production in 2019. It produced approximately 268,000 ounces of gold in 2023.

Complete Phase 3 underground mine development to access deeper ore zones
Transform Obuasi from a legacy operation into a modern, mechanized mine
Extend the mine life beyond 2040 through resource conversion and exploration
Implement world-class safety systems to achieve zero-harm targets
Create sustainable local economic benefits through preferential local procurement
technologies: Sub-level open stoping with paste backfill
Mechanized longhole drilling and blasting
Carbon-in-pulp (CIP) gold processing
Modern ventilation and refrigeration systems
Automated mine dispatch and fleet management
Tailings retreatment and sulphide flotation

milestones.title: Production Growth
milestones.description: Production reached approximately 268,000 ounces as underground development expanded.

milestones.title: Steady-State Target

projects.impact: The Obuasi redevelopment has created over 6,500 direct and contractor jobs, making it one of the largest employers in the Ashanti Region. The mine contributes significantly to Ghana's position as Africa's leading gold producer. AngloGold Ashanti has invested over $100 million in community development programs including education, healthcare, water supply, and alternative livelihood projects in the Obuasi municipality.

projects.name: Simandou Iron Ore Project
projects.tagline: The world's largest untapped high-grade iron ore deposit, poised to transform Guinea's economy.
projects.overview: Simandou is the world's largest known deposit of high-grade iron ore, located in the Simandou mountain range in southeastern Guinea. The project is divided into two concessions: Blocks 1 and 2 are held by the Winning Consortium Simandou (WCS), a joint venture led by Singapore-based Winning International Group and China's Weiqiao Aluminium; Blocks 3 and 4 are held by a Rio Tinto-led joint venture with Chalco Iron Ore Holdings and the Guinean government. The combined resource exceeds 2 billion tonnes at grades above 65% iron. A 670 km trans-Guinean railway and a deepwater port at Morebaya are being built to support exports.
objectives: Develop the world's largest greenfield iron ore mine with initial capacity of 60 Mtpa
Construct a 670 km trans-Guinean standard-gauge railway from the mine to the coast
Build a deepwater port at Morebaya capable of handling Capesize bulk carriers
Produce premium high-grade iron ore (65%+ Fe) to support steelmaking decarbonization
Generate transformative economic growth and infrastructure development for Guinea
Establish a co-development framework between WCS and Rio Tinto for shared infrastructure
technologies: Open-pit mining of high-grade hematite ore
Heavy-haul standard-gauge railway with 40-tonne axle loads
Deepwater port with ship-loading capacity for Capesize vessels
Ore processing through crushing, screening, and beneficiation
Environmental offset and biodiversity conservation programs
Remote sensing and geological modelling for mine planning

milestones.title: Discovery and Exploration

milestones.title: WCS Awarded Blocks 1 & 2

milestones.title: Co-Development Framework

milestones.title: Infrastructure Advancement

milestones.title: Target First Ore

resources.name: Morebaya Deepwater Port
resources.detail: A new deepwater port on the Guinea coast designed to load Capesize vessels for export to global steel markets.

resources.name: Mining Concessions
resources.detail: Blocks 1-2 held by WCS and Blocks 3-4 held by Rio Tinto consortium, together covering the full Simandou range.

projects.impact: Simandou is expected to generate over $15 billion in total investment and add an estimated 15-20% to Guinea's GDP once in full production. The project will create tens of thousands of construction and operational jobs and drive massive infrastructure development including roads, rail, ports, and power. It will position Guinea as a major global iron ore supplier and provide premium-grade ore critical to reducing steel industry carbon emissions.

projects.name: Syama Gold Mine
projects.tagline: The world's first purpose-built fully automated underground gold mine in southern Mali.
projects.overview: Syama is a gold mining complex in southern Mali owned and operated by Resolute Mining Limited. It comprises an underground mine, an open-pit oxide operation, and a sulphide processing plant. Syama made history as the world's first fully automated underground mine, deploying Sandvik's AutoMine system for autonomous loading and haulage. The mine produced approximately 226,000 ounces of gold in 2023 across both underground and oxide operations.
objectives: Operate the world's first fully automated underground gold mine
Optimize sulphide ore processing through the roaster and CIL plant
Maintain gold production above 200,000 ounces per year
Extend mine life through near-mine exploration and resource expansion
Reduce operating costs through automation and operational efficiency
Develop the Syama oxide deposits for additional low-cost production
technologies: Sandvik AutoMine fully autonomous underground loading and haulage
Sub-level open stoping mining method
Fluidized-bed roaster for refractory sulphide ore
Carbon-in-leach (CIL) gold recovery
Oxide heap leach processing
Autonomous teleremote operation centre

milestones.title: Stabilized Production

resources.name: Autonomous Underground Fleet

projects.impact: Syama employs over 2,800 people directly, with the vast majority being Malian nationals. The mine is the largest private employer in the Sikasso Region and has invested significantly in local infrastructure including roads, healthcare facilities, and education programs. Automation technology pioneered at Syama is setting the standard for the global mining industry's transition to autonomous operations.

projects.name: Platreef PGM Mine
projects.tagline: A next-generation platinum group metals mine on South Africa's Northern Limb of the Bushveld Complex.
projects.overview: Platreef is a large-scale underground platinum group metals (PGM), nickel, copper, and gold mine being developed by Ivanhoe Mines on the Northern Limb of the Bushveld Complex. The project is developed in three phases, with Phase 1 targeting 700,000 tonnes per annum. Shaft 1, a 996-metre production shaft, was completed in 2022, and the concentrator plant is under construction. Platreef is expected to become one of the largest PGM-producing mines in the world at full production, with an 87 Mtpa processing rate.
objectives: Develop a world-class PGM-nickel-copper-gold underground mine in three phases

projects.name: Tshipi Borwa Manganese Mine
projects.tagline: One of the world's lowest-cost manganese producers in the heart of South Africa's Kalahari Manganese Field.
projects.overview: Tshipi Borwa is a large open-pit manganese mine located in the Kalahari Manganese Field of South Africa's Northern Cape Province. It is jointly owned by Tshipi e Ntle Manganese Mining (Ntsimbintle Mining, Jupiter Mines, and other shareholders). The mine produces high-grade manganese ore (37-44% Mn) and has rapidly grown to become one of the world's largest and lowest-cost manganese producers. It exports primarily through the Port of Port Elizabeth (Gqeberha) and produced approximately 3.7 million tonnes in fiscal year 2023.

projects.name: Ambatovy Nickel Mine
projects.tagline: One of the world's largest laterite nickel mines, producing battery-grade nickel and cobalt in Madagascar.
projects.overview: Ambatovy is a large-scale laterite nickel and cobalt mining and processing operation in eastern Madagascar. The project involves an open-pit mine near Moramanga and a hydrometallurgical processing plant near Toamasina, connected by a 220 km slurry pipeline. It is majority-owned by Sumitomo Corporation and Korean consortium partners. With a design capacity of 60,000 tonnes of refined nickel and 5,600 tonnes of cobalt per year, Ambatovy is one of the largest laterite nickel operations outside of Indonesia and the Philippines.

projects.name: Husab Uranium Mine
projects.tagline: The world's second-largest uranium mine, powering the global nuclear energy supply from Namibia's Erongo Region.
projects.overview: Husab is a large-scale open-pit uranium mine located in the Namib Desert near Arandis, Namibia. It is operated by Swakop Uranium, a subsidiary of China General Nuclear Power Corporation (CGN), which holds a 90% stake with the Namibian government entity Epangelo Mining holding 10%. The mine commenced production in 2016 and has ramped up to become the world's second-largest uranium-producing mine. It produced approximately 3,400 tonnes of uranium oxide (U3O8) in 2023.

projects.name: Liqhobong Diamond Mine
projects.tagline: A high-altitude kimberlite diamond mine producing exceptional gem-quality stones in the Lesotho Highlands.
projects.overview: Liqhobong is an open-pit diamond mine situated at approximately 2,500 metres above sea level in the Maluti Mountains of Lesotho. It is operated by Firestone Diamonds (75%), with the Government of Lesotho holding a 25% free-carried interest. The mine processes the Main Pipe kimberlite and has been in production since 2016. Liqhobong is known for producing large, high-quality gemstone diamonds, with notable stones exceeding 100 carats recovered. The mine processes approximately 3.6 million tonnes of kimberlite per year.

projects.name: Konkola Copper Mines
projects.tagline: Zambia's deepest and most significant copper mining operation in the heart of the Copperbelt.
projects.overview: Konkola Copper Mines (KCM) is one of Zambia's largest integrated copper mining and smelting operations, comprising the Konkola underground mine, Nchanga open-pit and underground mines, and the Nkana smelter and refinery. Following years of operational challenges under Vedanta Resources' ownership, the Zambian government placed KCM under provisional liquidation in 2019. In 2023, International Resources Holding (IRH) of Abu Dhabi acquired a 51% stake, with the Zambian government retaining a significant interest. KCM has historically produced over 100,000 tonnes of copper annually.

projects.tagline: B2Gold's flagship gold mine, one of the lowest-cost large gold producers in the world.
projects.overview: Fekola is a large open-pit gold mine in southwestern Mali, owned and operated by B2Gold Corp (80%) with the Government of Mali holding 20%. The mine was built on time and under budget, commencing production in 2017. Fekola has rapidly become one of the world's lowest-cost large gold mines, producing approximately 589,000 ounces in 2023. A major expansion completed in 2022 increased processing capacity from 7.5 Mtpa to 10 Mtpa. B2Gold has also commissioned a 30 MW solar plant to power the mine, one of the largest off-grid solar installations in West Africa.

Operate as one of the world's lowest-cost large gold producers
Maximize the expanded 10 Mtpa processing plant capacity
Integrate the 30 MW solar power plant to reduce carbon emissions and fuel costs
Extend mine life through satellite pit development at Cardinal and Anaconda Area
Continue responsible mining with strong community and environmental programs

Carbon-in-leach (CIL) gold recovery
Gravity concentration for coarse gold
30 MW solar photovoltaic power plant with battery storage

projects.name: Sukari Gold Mine
projects.tagline: Egypt's first and only modern large-scale gold mine, producing over 400,000 ounces annually in the Eastern Desert.
projects.overview: Sukari is Egypt's first large-scale modern gold mine, located in the Eastern Desert approximately 700 km south of Cairo and 25 km from the Red Sea coast. It is operated by Centamin plc under a 50/50 profit-sharing agreement with the Egyptian Mineral Resource Authority (EMRA). The mine comprises a large open pit, an expanding underground operation, and a 12.5 Mtpa processing plant. Sukari produced approximately 450,000 ounces of gold in 2023 and has cumulative production exceeding 5 million ounces since commencing operations in 2009.

Expand underground mining to sustain production as the open pit matures
Extend mine life beyond 2035 through exploration success and resource conversion
Optimize the 12.5 Mtpa processing plant for maximum gold recovery
Maintain Sukari as the sole large-scale gold mine in Egypt
Invest in renewable energy to reduce the mine's carbon footprint

projects.name: Kamoto Copper Mine
projects.tagline: One of the world's largest copper-cobalt producers, anchoring Glencore's operations in the DRC's Katanga province.
projects.overview: Kamoto Copper Company (KCC) operates one of the largest copper-cobalt mining complexes in the world, located near Kolwezi in the DRC's Lualaba Province. The operation is 75% owned by Glencore (through its subsidiary Katanga Mining) and 25% by state-owned Gecamines. KCC comprises the Kamoto underground mine, the KOV and Mashamba East open pits, a concentrator, and a modern hydrometallurgical processing plant. The complex produced approximately 250,000 tonnes of copper cathode and significant cobalt output in 2023, making it one of the DRC's largest mining operations.

Implement responsible sourcing standards for cobalt traceability

projects.name: Mombasa–Nairobi Standard Gauge Railway (SGR)
projects.tagline: A 472 km modern railway connecting Kenya's largest port city to its capital in under five hours.
projects.overview: The Mombasa–Nairobi Standard Gauge Railway is a flagship railway project built to replace the century-old metre-gauge line connecting Kenya's two largest cities. The 472 km line was constructed by China Road and Bridge Corporation (CRBC) at a cost of approximately $3.8 billion, making it the most expensive infrastructure project in Kenyan history. The railway operates the Madaraka Express passenger service, reducing travel time from over 10 hours to roughly 4.5 hours, and carries significant freight volumes previously transported by road.

Reduce travel time between Mombasa and Nairobi from 10+ hours to under 5 hours
Shift freight transport from congested roads to rail, targeting 40% of corridor freight
Lower the cost of goods transport to boost regional competitiveness
Serve as Phase 1 of a broader East African railway network linking to Uganda, Rwanda, and South Sudan
Create employment opportunities during construction and long-term operations

Chinese CR-type diesel locomotives

Centralized Traffic Control (CTC) system

The project created an estimated 46,000 jobs during construction, with over 25,000 going to Kenyan workers. Freight costs on the corridor have dropped by approximately 60% compared to road haulage, boosting Kenya's trade competitiveness.

projects.name: Lekki Deep Sea Port
projects.tagline: Nigeria's first deep-sea port, designed to become West Africa's premier maritime gateway.
projects.overview: The Lekki Deep Sea Port is Nigeria's first deep-water port, located within the Lekki Free Trade Zone approximately 65 km east of Lagos city centre. Built at a cost of around $1.5 billion, the port features a 16.5-metre draught capable of receiving the largest container vessels. Officially inaugurated in January 2023, it is operated by Lekki Freeport Terminal under a 45-year concession and is designed to handle 2.7 million TEU of containers at full build-out, dramatically reducing Nigeria's reliance on the congested Apapa and Tin Can Island ports.

Reduce congestion at Lagos's existing Apapa and Tin Can Island ports
Serve as an economic anchor for the Lekki Free Trade Zone, attracting manufacturing and logistics investment
Capture trans-shipment traffic currently going to ports in Togo, Ghana, and Côte d'Ivoire
Create thousands of direct and indirect jobs in Lagos State
Boost Nigeria's non-oil export capacity and reduce import costs

The port has already begun reducing truck congestion in Lagos by diverting cargo away from the heavily burdened Apapa corridor.

projects.name: Addis Ababa Light Rail Transit
projects.tagline: Sub-Saharan Africa's first modern light rail system, carrying over 100,000 passengers daily in Ethiopia's capital.
projects.overview: The Addis Ababa Light Rail Transit (AA-LRT) is sub-Saharan Africa's first operational light rail system. Built by the China Railway Group at a cost of $475 million, the system comprises two lines totalling 31.6 km with 39 stations. Opened in September 2015, it was designed to carry up to 60,000 passengers per hour per direction and has significantly eased traffic congestion in one of Africa's fastest-growing capitals, which has a population exceeding 5 million.

Reduce severe road congestion and commute times across the capital
Lower transportation costs for low- and middle-income residents
Reduce vehicle emissions and improve urban air quality
Serve as a catalyst for transit-oriented urban development
Demonstrate the viability of modern rail transit in sub-Saharan Africa

projects.name: Lagos–Ibadan Expressway Reconstruction
projects.tagline: A 127 km reconstruction of Nigeria's busiest highway, carrying over 250,000 vehicles daily between Lagos and Ibadan.
projects.overview: The Lagos–Ibadan Expressway is the most heavily trafficked highway in Nigeria, connecting the commercial capital Lagos to Ibadan, the third-largest city. Originally built in the 1970s, the 127.6 km dual carriageway had deteriorated severely. A major reconstruction project was awarded to Julius Berger Nigeria and Reynolds Construction Company (RCC) in 2013, with the scope expanded to include widening from 4 to 6 lanes in many sections. The project cost has escalated to over $1.5 billion and has seen multiple completion date revisions.

Reduce travel time between Lagos and Ibadan from 3-4 hours to under 90 minutes
Improve road safety by eliminating dangerous sections and adding modern features
Reduce vehicle operating costs for the 250,000+ daily users
Construct new interchanges and flyovers at major intersections
Support economic integration of the Lagos-Ibadan-Oyo industrial corridor

projects.name: Maputo–Katembe Bridge (Maputo Bay Bridge)
projects.tagline: Africa's longest suspension bridge, spanning 3 km across Maputo Bay to connect Mozambique's capital to its southern hinterland.
projects.overview: The Maputo–Katembe Bridge, officially the Maputo Bay Bridge, is a cable-stayed bridge spanning Maputo Bay in Mozambique. At approximately 3,000 metres in total length with a main span of 680 metres and a clearance of 60 metres above the water, it is one of the longest cable-stayed bridges in Africa. Built by China Road and Bridge Corporation at a cost of around $785 million (including approach roads), the bridge was inaugurated in November 2018. It connects the capital Maputo to the Katembe district and onward to South Africa, replacing a ferry that could take hours.

Eliminate dependence on the slow and limited Maputo Bay ferry service
Open southern Mozambique to tourism and economic development
Improve road connectivity to South Africa via the EN1 highway
Stimulate urban development and land value in the Katembe area
Reduce travel time from several hours by ferry to minutes by road

projects.name: Tanger Med Port Complex
projects.tagline: Africa's largest port by container throughput, strategically positioned at the Strait of Gibraltar.
projects.overview: Tanger Med is a major port complex on the Strait of Gibraltar in northern Morocco, approximately 40 km east of Tangier. It is Africa's largest port by container throughput and ranks among the top 25 globally, handling over 9 million TEU annually following the opening of Tanger Med 2 in 2019. The complex includes container terminals, a vehicle terminal, a passenger and ferry terminal, and free trade zones. The total investment across all phases exceeds $8 billion. Operated primarily by APM Terminals, Eurogate, and Marsa Maroc, it serves as a major trans-shipment hub linking Africa, Europe, and the Americas.

Diversify Morocco's economy beyond agriculture and phosphates

projects.name: New Suez Canal
projects.tagline: A $8.2 billion expansion doubling the capacity of one of the world's most critical waterways.
projects.overview: The New Suez Canal project involved the construction of a 35 km new parallel channel alongside the existing Suez Canal and the widening and deepening of another 37 km of the existing waterway. Completed in just one year at a cost of $8.2 billion, entirely funded by Egyptian investment certificates, the project was inaugurated in August 2015. It enables two-way traffic through much of the canal, reducing transit waiting times from an average of 18 hours to 11 hours and increasing the canal's daily ship capacity from 49 to 97 vessels.

Increase daily vessel capacity from 49 to 97 ships

Advanced vessel traffic management systems

projects.name: Nairobi Expressway
projects.tagline: Kenya's first urban toll expressway, cutting through Nairobi's notorious traffic in minutes instead of hours.
projects.overview: The Nairobi Expressway is a 27.1 km elevated toll highway running from Mlolongo (near Jomo Kenyatta International Airport) to Westlands in Nairobi. Built by China Road and Bridge Corporation under a Public-Private Partnership with the Kenyan government at a cost of approximately $668 million, the expressway was opened to traffic in July 2022. It features 10 interchanges and uses electronic toll collection, reducing travel time on the corridor from up to 2 hours during peak times to approximately 20 minutes. It is operated under a 30-year concession by Moja Expressway.

Reduce commute times from Jomo Kenyatta International Airport to the Westlands business district

Reduce fuel consumption and vehicle emissions from idling in traffic
Provide reliable journey times for airport passengers and freight

projects.name: Abidjan Metro (Métro d'Abidjan)
projects.tagline: West Africa's first metro system, a 37 km line set to transform urban mobility in Côte d'Ivoire's economic capital.
projects.overview: The Abidjan Metro is a 37.5 km metro line under construction connecting Anyama in the north to the Félix Houphouët-Boigny International Airport in the south. When completed, it will be West Africa's first metro system. The project, estimated at over €1.7 billion (approximately $1.9 billion), is being built by a consortium led by Bouygues and Keolis, with financing from the French Development Agency (AFD), the World Bank, and the European Investment Bank. The line will feature 18 stations and is designed to carry 500,000 passengers per day.

projects.name: Trans-Saharan Highway
projects.tagline: A 4,500 km highway corridor crossing the Sahara Desert to link North Africa with West Africa's markets.
projects.country: Algeria, Niger, Nigeria, Tunisia, Chad, Mali
projects.overview: The Trans-Saharan Highway is a network of paved roads spanning approximately 4,500 km from Algiers, Algeria to Lagos, Nigeria, crossing the Sahara Desert. Part of the broader Trans-African Highway network envisioned by the African Union, the highway has been under development since the 1960s. The main corridor passes through Algeria, Niger, and Nigeria, with branches to Tunisia, Chad, and Mali. While substantial portions are now paved, some sections particularly in northern Niger remain incomplete or poorly maintained, and the project continues to receive investment from the African Development Bank and participating governments.
objectives: Create a continuous paved road link between North Africa and West Africa
Facilitate trade between Algeria, Niger, Nigeria, and neighbouring countries
Reduce the isolation of Saharan communities and improve access to services
Support economic integration as envisioned by the African Continental Free Trade Area
Provide an alternative to sea routes for North-South African trade
Improve security and governance in remote Saharan regions through connectivity
technologies: Desert-grade asphalt and concrete road construction
Sand dune stabilisation and erosion control
Solar-powered road lighting at key points
Rest stations with water and fuel in remote sections
GPS-based navigation aids for desert crossings
Border post modernisation for customs facilitation

projects.impact: Completed sections have already transformed trade flows, enabling trucks to transport goods between North and West Africa in days rather than weeks. The highway supports millions of people in Saharan towns along the route. Full completion could increase bilateral trade between Nigeria and Algeria by an estimated 300% and provide a vital lifeline for landlocked Niger. The project is a cornerstone of the African Union's Programme for Infrastructure Development in Africa (PIDA).

projects.name: Lamu Port (LAPSSET Corridor)
projects.tagline: Kenya's ambitious second deep-water port, the gateway to the $25 billion LAPSSET infrastructure corridor.
projects.overview: Lamu Port is the anchor project of the Lamu Port-South Sudan-Ethiopia Transport (LAPSSET) Corridor, one of Africa's most ambitious infrastructure programmes. Located on Manda Bay near the historic town of Lamu, the port is being developed with a planned 32 berths across three phases. The first three berths were completed with the first berth inaugurated in 2021. The project cost for the full port is estimated at over $5 billion, with the broader LAPSSET corridor including highways, railways, a pipeline, and resort cities valued at $25 billion. The port is designed to handle containers, bulk cargo, and oil exports.

Create a transport corridor connecting Kenya to South Sudan and Ethiopia
Support future crude oil exports from South Sudan and Kenya's Turkana oilfields
Open up northern Kenya to economic development and reduce regional inequality
Position Kenya as a logistics hub for the Horn of Africa and Great Lakes region
Handle up to 23.5 million tonnes of cargo annually at full capacity

projects.name: Walvis Bay Port Expansion
projects.tagline: Namibia's $300 million port expansion creating a new container terminal to serve Southern Africa's Atlantic trade.
projects.overview: The Walvis Bay Port Expansion project involved the construction of a new container terminal on a 40-hectare reclaimed land area, expanding Namibia's main port capacity dramatically. Completed in August 2019 at a cost of approximately $300 million, the project increased the port's container handling capacity from 350,000 TEU to 750,000 TEU annually. Financed by the African Development Bank and Namport (Namibian Ports Authority), the expansion included a 600-metre quay wall, deepened berths to 16 metres, and modern container handling equipment. It positions Walvis Bay as a logistics hub for Southern African landlocked countries including Botswana, Zambia, and Zimbabwe.

Reduce cargo dwell times and improve port efficiency

projects.overview: Konza Technopolis, often called 'Silicon Savannah,' is a planned smart city and technology hub being developed on 5,000 acres (2,023 hectares) of land in Machakos County, approximately 60 km south of Nairobi. projects.tagline: Tanzania's landmark cable-stayed bridge combined with its transformative Standard Gauge Railway project.
projects.region: Dar es Salaam and Central Tanzania
projects.overview: Tanzania has embarked on two transformative transport projects. The Kigamboni Bridge (originally proposed as the Julius Nyerere Bridge) is a 680-metre cable-stayed bridge across the Kurasini Creek in Dar es Salaam, connecting the city centre to the Kigamboni district. More significantly, Tanzania is building a 1,219 km Standard Gauge Railway from Dar es Salaam to Mwanza on Lake Victoria, with a branch to Isaka. The SGR is being constructed in five lots at an estimated cost of $11.7 billion, with Lot 1 (Dar es Salaam to Morogoro, 300 km) inaugurated in 2024 and further sections under construction. The SGR will eventually connect to neighbouring countries' rail networks.

Serve passenger traffic between Tanzania's major cities
Support the development of the Kigamboni New City area via the bridge

When fully completed, the railway will dramatically reduce the cost of moving freight from Dar es Salaam port to landlocked countries, with estimated savings of 40% compared to road transport. The project has created over 30,000 construction jobs. The Kigamboni Bridge has opened Dar es Salaam's last major undeveloped district to growth.

projects.name: Dakar–Diamniadio Toll Highway (Autoroute à Péage)
projects.tagline: Senegal's first modern toll motorway, cutting the trip from Dakar to its new administrative capital from 2 hours to 30 minutes.
projects.overview: The Dakar–Diamniadio Toll Highway is Senegal's first modern motorway, a 32 km dual carriageway connecting central Dakar to the new city of Diamniadio and onward to the Blaise Diagne International Airport. Built under a PPP concession at a cost of approximately €380 million ($430 million), the highway was completed in phases between 2013 and 2016. It features 6 lanes in some sections and electronic toll collection. The highway has been extended to the airport (AIBD), creating a 48 km total corridor. It is operated by SENAC (Société d'Exploitation de l'Autoroute à Péage), a concession held by Eiffage.
objectives: Provide a modern motorway link between Dakar and the new administrative city of Diamniadio
Reduce crippling traffic congestion on the main corridor out of Dakar
Connect the capital to Blaise Diagne International Airport via a high-speed road
Introduce modern toll road infrastructure and PPP financing in Senegal
Support the development of Diamniadio as Senegal's new administrative and economic hub
Reduce travel time from 2+ hours to under 30 minutes
technologies: Modern toll motorway with electronic toll collection
6-lane divided highway (4+2 configuration)
Grade-separated interchanges
Modern highway drainage and safety barriers
Intelligent transport system with CCTV monitoring
Noise barriers in urban sections

milestones.title: Phase 1 Opens

milestones.title: Full Highway Opens

milestones.title: Further Extensions Planned

It has been the catalyst for the rapid development of Diamniadio new city, which now hosts the Abdou Diouf International Conference Centre, government ministries, and major commercial developments. The project created over 4,000 construction jobs and serves as a model PPP transaction for West Africa.

projects.name: Kribi Deep Sea Port
projects.tagline: Cameroon's first deep-water port, a $1.3 billion gateway to Central Africa's resource-rich hinterland.
projects.overview: The Kribi Deep Sea Port is Cameroon's first deep-water port, located approximately 150 km south of Douala. Phase 1, completed in 2018 at a cost of $567 million, includes a multipurpose terminal and a container terminal with a 16-metre draught. Phase 2, estimated at over $700 million, will add additional container and hydrocarbon berths. The port serves as an export terminal for iron ore from the Mbalam-Nabeba project and handles general cargo for landlocked Central African countries including Chad and the Central African Republic. It is operated by Kribi Conteneurs Terminal (KCT), a Bolloré subsidiary.
objectives: Provide Cameroon with a modern deep-water port to complement the congested Douala port
Handle mineral exports from Cameroon's mining sector, particularly iron ore
Serve as a gateway for landlocked Central African Republic and Chad
Accommodate larger vessels that cannot access the shallow Douala port
Develop a port-centric industrial zone to attract manufacturing investment
Increase Cameroon's port competitiveness in the Gulf of Guinea
technologies: 16 m deep-water berths for Capesize and Post-Panamax vessels
Ship-to-shore gantry cranes
Multipurpose and container terminal equipment
1,355 m main breakwater for wave protection
Connected highway to Douala-Kribi corridor
Oil and gas pipeline terminal connection (planned)

It handles growing volumes of containers and bulk cargo, serving landlocked neighbours. Phase 1 created approximately 5,000 construction jobs, and the port directly employs over 500 workers. When the mining corridor is fully operational, Kribi will export millions of tonnes of iron ore annually, potentially transforming Cameroon's export profile.

projects.name: Mombasa–Nairobi Expressway (A109 Upgrade)
projects.tagline: A proposed $3 billion expressway upgrading Kenya's vital 500 km Mombasa–Nairobi highway corridor.
projects.overview: The Mombasa–Nairobi Expressway is a proposed upgrade of the existing 500 km A109 highway connecting Kenya's two largest cities. Distinct from the Nairobi urban expressway and the SGR, this project envisions transforming the current 2-lane highway into a modern 4-6 lane divided expressway. Various proposals have been evaluated, including a Bechtel-led plan estimated at $3 billion and alternative designs. The corridor carries the majority of Kenya's freight and passenger traffic between the coast and the interior. While the project has been in planning for years, it remains a top priority under Kenya's transport development agenda given the deteriorating condition of the existing road.
objectives: Upgrade the critical Mombasa–Nairobi corridor from a 2-lane road to a 4-6 lane expressway
Reduce road fatalities on one of Kenya's most dangerous highways
Decrease travel time from 8-10 hours to approximately 4-5 hours
Complement the SGR by providing a modern road alternative for freight and passengers
Reduce vehicle operating costs on the corridor by improving road quality
Stimulate economic development in towns along the route
technologies: Modern divided expressway with controlled access
Grade-separated interchanges at major junctions
Electronic toll collection systems
Highway management and incident response systems
Wildlife crossings through Tsavo ecosystem
Service centres and rest areas along the route

milestones.title: Procurement Preparation

The existing road accounts for a disproportionate share of Kenya's road accidents. The project is expected to create over 50,000 construction jobs and reduce freight costs by an estimated 30%, significantly boosting the competitiveness of goods passing through the Port of Mombasa.

projects.name: Dangote Fertiliser Plant
projects.tagline: Africa's largest urea fertiliser plant — boosting food security across the continent.
projects.overview: The Dangote Fertiliser Plant is a $2.5 billion granulated urea production facility located adjacent to the Dangote Refinery in the Lekki Free Trade Zone, Lagos. With an installed capacity of 3 million metric tonnes per annum (MTPA), it is the largest urea fertiliser plant in Africa and one of the largest globally. The plant converts natural gas into ammonia and then urea, using Haldor Topsoe and Saipem technology. Since commencing production in 2022, it has transformed Nigeria from a fertiliser importer into a net exporter, shipping to markets across Africa, India, and Latin America.
objectives: Produce 3 million metric tonnes of urea fertiliser per year to supply domestic and export markets
Reduce Nigeria's dependence on imported fertiliser and save foreign exchange reserves
Boost agricultural productivity across West Africa by ensuring affordable fertiliser supply
Create over 5,000 direct jobs and 25,000 indirect jobs in manufacturing, logistics, and agriculture
Generate significant export revenue by shipping urea to Brazil, India, Mexico, and other African countries
Monetise Nigeria's abundant natural gas reserves through value-added industrial production
technologies: Haldor Topsoe SynCOR ammonia synthesis technology for high-efficiency gas-to-ammonia conversion
Saipem urea granulation technology producing premium-grade granulated urea for global markets
Snamprogetti urea synthesis process with high energy efficiency and low emissions profile
Dedicated natural gas pipeline infrastructure for feedstock supply from Nigerian gas fields
Automated bagging and palletising systems with 3,000 MTPD capacity for rapid product dispatch
On-site effluent treatment and emissions control systems meeting World Bank environmental standards

milestones.title: Construction Commencement
milestones.description: Full-scale construction begins at the Lekki Free Trade Zone site with Saipem as the main EPC contractor. Over 10,000 workers deployed during peak construction.

milestones.title: Mechanical Completion
milestones.description: Main process units including ammonia and urea trains reach mechanical completion. Pre-commissioning activities begin despite COVID-19 pandemic disruptions.

milestones.title: First Ammonia Production
milestones.description: The ammonia synthesis train produces its first output, marking a critical milestone. Ammonia is the key intermediate feedstock for urea production.

milestones.title: Commercial Urea Production Begins
milestones.description: The plant achieves commercial production of granulated urea and begins domestic sales. First export cargoes dispatched to international buyers in Brazil and India.

milestones.title: Full Capacity Operations
milestones.description: Plant reaches nameplate capacity of 3 million MTPA. Nigeria records first-ever trade surplus in fertiliser. Over 1 million tonnes exported in the first full year of operation.

milestones.title: Pan-African Distribution Expansion
milestones.description: Expansion of distribution networks across 15 African countries through partnerships with national agricultural agencies and private distributors.

resources.name: Natural Gas Feedstock
resources.detail: Approximately 70 million standard cubic feet per day of natural gas consumed as feedstock, sourced from Nigerian gas producers via dedicated pipeline.

resources.name: Ammonia Synthesis Unit
resources.detail: Single-train ammonia plant producing approximately 4,000 tonnes per day of anhydrous ammonia as intermediate feedstock for urea production.

resources.name: Urea Granulation Plant
resources.detail: Converts liquid urea into granulated form suitable for agricultural application and long-distance transport. Output capacity of 3 million MTPA.

resources.name: Deep-Sea Port Access
resources.detail: Direct access to the Lekki Deep Sea Port for bulk export of urea via dedicated jetty facilities, enabling efficient loading of Panamax vessels.

resources.name: Bagging and Logistics Facility
resources.detail: Automated 50 kg bagging lines with total capacity of 3,000 tonnes per day. On-site warehousing for 100,000 tonnes of finished product.

projects.impact: The Dangote Fertiliser Plant has turned Nigeria into Africa's largest fertiliser producer and a net exporter, saving the country over $500 million annually in import costs. It supplies affordable urea to farmers across West Africa, directly supporting food security for over 200 million people. The plant has created approximately 30,000 direct and indirect jobs and generates significant export revenue from shipments to over 20 countries.

projects.name: Ajaokuta Steel Company Limited
projects.tagline: Nigeria's incomplete steel dream — the largest integrated steel complex in sub-Saharan Africa.
projects.overview: The Ajaokuta Steel Company Limited is an integrated steel plant located on 24,000 hectares in Kogi State, Nigeria. Originally constructed by the Soviet firm Tyazhpromexport starting in 1979, the plant was designed to produce 1.3 million tonnes of liquid steel annually using blast furnace technology. Despite reaching approximately 98% mechanical completion, the plant has never achieved sustained commercial production due to decades of political mismanagement, contractual disputes, and funding shortfalls. As of 2025, the Nigerian government has initiated renewed efforts to complete and commission the facility through international partnerships.
objectives: Produce 1.3 million tonnes per annum of liquid steel for domestic construction and manufacturing sectors
Reduce Nigeria's $3.3 billion annual steel import bill by substituting domestic production
Supply long products including billets, wire rods, and reinforcement bars for infrastructure development
Create over 10,000 direct jobs and catalyse industrialisation of the Kogi State region
Establish Nigeria as a primary steel producer in West Africa reducing regional import dependency
Develop downstream steel fabrication industries using Ajaokuta output as feedstock
technologies: Blast furnace ironmaking technology with two 1,000 cubic metre furnaces designed by Tyazhpromexport
Basic oxygen steelmaking (BOF) converters for primary steel production from pig iron
Continuous casting machines for producing billets and blooms from liquid steel
Light section rolling mill, wire rod mill, and medium section mill for finished product manufacturing
Coke oven battery with by-product recovery plant for converting coal into metallurgical coke
Sintering plant for agglomerating iron ore fines into blast furnace feed material

milestones.title: Construction Begins
milestones.description: The Nigerian government signs a contract with the Soviet firm Tyazhpromexport to build the integrated steel plant. Construction commences on the 24,000-hectare site along the Niger River.

milestones.title: First Rolling Mill Commissioned
milestones.description: The light section rolling mill is commissioned and produces its first steel products using imported billets, demonstrating partial capability of the downstream facilities.

milestones.title: Construction Effectively Halted
milestones.description: Chronic funding shortfalls and political instability lead to virtual cessation of construction activities. The plant is reported at 98% mechanical completion but key systems remain unfinished.

milestones.title: Global Infrastructure Concession
milestones.description: The Nigerian government grants a 10-year concession to Global Infrastructure Holdings (Solgas) to complete and operate the plant. The concession is later terminated in 2008 amid disputes.

milestones.title: Renewed Federal Government Initiative
milestones.description: The Nigerian government appropriates fresh funds and begins technical audits of the facility to determine the scope of work needed for completion. Multiple international firms express interest.

milestones.title: Completion Partnership Negotiations
milestones.description: Ongoing negotiations with international partners including firms from Russia, China, and India to finance and complete the remaining work. Target commissioning timeline remains under discussion.

resources.name: Iron Ore Supply
resources.detail: Designed to use iron ore from Itakpe deposits in Kogi State, located approximately 60 km away and connected by a dedicated rail line operated by the National Iron Ore Mining Company.

resources.name: Coking Coal
resources.detail: Metallurgical coal for coke production to be sourced from international suppliers and potentially from Nigerian coal deposits in Enugu State.

resources.name: Blast Furnace Complex
resources.detail: Two blast furnaces with combined capacity of 1.3 million tonnes per year of pig iron. Requires completion of auxiliary systems before commissioning.

resources.name: Rolling Mills
resources.detail: Three rolling mills — light section, medium section, and wire rod — for producing finished steel products. The light section mill has been test-operated in the past.

resources.name: 200 MW Captive Power Plant
resources.detail: Gas-fired power plant to supply the steelworks with electricity. Currently non-operational and requires rehabilitation.

projects.impact: If completed, Ajaokuta Steel would save Nigeria an estimated $3.3 billion annually in steel imports and create over 500,000 direct and indirect jobs. The plant would anchor an entire industrial corridor in central Nigeria, catalysing downstream industries in construction, automotive, and manufacturing. Despite over $8 billion invested to date, the plant remains one of Africa's most prominent examples of stalled industrialisation, making its potential completion a transformative economic event.

facts.capacity: 1.3 million tonnes per annum of liquid steel (design capacity)
facts.investment: Over $8 billion invested since 1979 with additional billions needed for completion
facts.size: 24,000 hectares (240 square kilometres) making it one of the largest industrial sites in Africa
facts.jobs: Projected 10,000 direct jobs and 500,000 indirect jobs when fully operational
facts.location: Ajaokuta, Kogi State, on the banks of the Niger River, central Nigeria
facts.status: 98% mechanically complete but never achieved sustained commercial production
facts.owner: Federal Government of Nigeria through the Ministry of Mines and Steel Development

projects.name: Volkswagen Rwanda Assembly Plant
projects.tagline: East Africa's first modern car assembly plant — putting Rwanda on the automotive map.
projects.overview: Volkswagen Rwanda is a vehicle assembly and mobility services operation established in Kigali in 2018, marking the first time Volkswagen assembled vehicles in East Africa. The plant assembles Polo, Tiguan, and Passat models using semi-knocked-down (SKD) kits imported from VW's South African plant. Beyond assembly, VW Rwanda pioneered a car-sharing mobility service called Move, one of the first of its kind in Africa, using a smartphone app to provide affordable ride-sharing. The operation serves as VW's testbed for new mobility models in emerging African markets.
objectives: Establish a modern vehicle assembly capability in East Africa starting with semi-knocked-down kit assembly
Develop and test new urban mobility solutions including car-sharing services for African cities
Build local automotive skills and a trained workforce pipeline for Rwanda's industrialisation goals
Support Rwanda's Vision 2050 goal of becoming a middle-income knowledge-based economy
Demonstrate the viability of automotive manufacturing in East Africa to attract further investment
technologies: Semi-knocked-down (SKD) vehicle assembly process with models imported from VWSA Uitenhage plant
Move by Volkswagen — smartphone-based car-sharing and ride-hailing platform for urban mobility
Quality management systems compliant with VW Group's global production standards
Digital vehicle tracking and fleet management systems for the mobility services operation
Training and skills development programmes in automotive assembly and maintenance

milestones.description: Volkswagen Group signs a memorandum of understanding with the Government of Rwanda to explore vehicle assembly and new mobility services in Kigali.

milestones.description: VW Rwanda officially opens its assembly facility in the Kigali Special Economic Zone. First locally assembled Polo rolls off the line. VW becomes the first global automaker to assemble cars in East Africa.

milestones.title: Move Car-Sharing Service Launches

milestones.title: Production Scale-Up

milestones.title: Regional Market Expansion
milestones.description: VW Rwanda explores supplying assembled vehicles to neighbouring East African Community markets including DR Congo, Burundi, and Uganda.

resources.name: SKD Assembly Kits

resources.name: Assembly Facility

resources.name: Trained Workforce

projects.impact: VW Rwanda demonstrated that modern vehicle assembly is viable in East Africa, inspiring similar investments by other automakers. The Move car-sharing service provided affordable urban mobility to thousands of Kigali residents. The plant has trained hundreds of Rwandan technicians in automotive manufacturing skills and directly contributed to Rwanda's industrial GDP. It positioned Kigali as a hub for automotive and mobility innovation in the region.

facts.investment: Approximately $20 million initial investment by Volkswagen Group
