projects.name: Mozal Aluminium Smelter
projects.tagline: Southern Africa's largest aluminium smelter — transforming Mozambique's industrial landscape.
projects.overview: Mozal (Mozambique Aluminium) is a large-scale aluminium smelter located in the Beluluane Industrial Park near Maputo, Mozambique. Originally a joint venture between BHP (now South32), Mitsubishi, the Industrial Development Corporation of South Africa, and the Government of Mozambique, the smelter was built in two phases. Phase I commenced production in 2000 and Phase II in 2003, bringing total capacity to approximately 563,000 tonnes of primary aluminium per year. The smelter draws power from the Cahora Bassa hydroelectric dam and uses alumina imported primarily from Australia. Mozal is the single largest industrial project in Mozambique's history and accounts for a significant share of the country's export revenue.
objectives: Produce over 560,000 tonnes per year of primary aluminium ingots for global export markets
Leverage Mozambique's abundant low-cost hydroelectric power for energy-intensive industrial production
Diversify Mozambique's economy beyond agriculture and extractive resources
Create sustained employment and skills development in modern heavy industry
Generate significant export revenue and government fiscal receipts for Mozambique
Serve as an anchor investment to attract further industrial development to the Beluluane Industrial Park
technologies: Hall-Heroult electrolytic reduction process with 1,080 prebaked anode reduction cells across two potlines
Prebaked anode manufacturing facility producing carbon anodes on-site for the reduction process
Cast house with multiple furnaces producing standard ingots, T-bars, and sow for export
Advanced potline process control systems for optimising energy efficiency and aluminium quality
Fume treatment centres with dry scrubbing systems for fluoride emission recovery and environmental control
Direct current power supply infrastructure drawing from the Mozambique national grid (Cahora Bassa hydro)

milestones.title: Phase I Construction Begins
milestones.description: Construction of the first phase of the smelter begins in the Beluluane Industrial Park. Estimated cost of Phase I is $1.34 billion. BHP leads the consortium.

milestones.title: Phase I First Metal Production
milestones.description: Mozal produces its first aluminium in June 2000, just 26 months after construction started — a record for a greenfield smelter. Phase I capacity of approximately 253,000 tonnes per year.

milestones.title: Phase II Completion and Full Capacity
milestones.description: Phase II doubles smelter capacity to approximately 563,000 tonnes per year with the addition of a second potline. Total investment across both phases reaches $2.4 billion.

milestones.title: Transfer to South32
milestones.description: BHP Billiton demerges its non-core assets into a new company called South32, which takes over BHP's majority stake in Mozal. South32 becomes the primary operator.

milestones.title: Operational Optimisation
milestones.description: Mozal implements potline modernisation programs to improve current efficiency and reduce energy consumption per tonne of aluminium produced.

milestones.title: Continued Operations and Sustainability Focus
milestones.description: Mozal continues operating near nameplate capacity, contributing approximately 60% of Mozambique's manufacturing exports. Focus on renewable energy credentials given hydro-powered production.

resources.name: Alumina Feedstock
resources.detail: Approximately 1.1 million tonnes per year of alumina imported primarily from Australia, delivered by bulk carrier to dedicated port facilities.

resources.name: Hydroelectric Power
resources.detail: Approximately 900 MW of electricity drawn from the Mozambique grid, predominantly sourced from the 2,075 MW Cahora Bassa hydroelectric dam on the Zambezi River.

resources.name: Reduction Potlines
resources.detail: Two potlines containing a total of 1,080 prebaked anode reduction cells operating at approximately 350 kA current intensity.

resources.name: Anode Plant
resources.detail: On-site anode manufacturing facility producing prebaked carbon anodes from petroleum coke and coal tar pitch.

resources.name: Dedicated Port and Rail
resources.detail: Access to Maputo port facilities for alumina import and aluminium export. Connected by rail and road to the port.

projects.impact: Mozal transformed Mozambique's industrial economy, accounting for approximately 7% of GDP and over 50% of total export revenue at peak. The smelter directly employs approximately 1,200 workers with over 90% being Mozambican nationals, and supports thousands of indirect jobs. Mozal was instrumental in developing the Beluluane Industrial Park into a major industrial zone and demonstrated the viability of large-scale industrial investment in post-conflict Mozambique. Its hydro-powered production gives it one of the lowest carbon footprints of any primary aluminium smelter globally.

facts.capacity: 563,000 tonnes per year of primary aluminium
facts.investment: $2.4 billion across Phase I and Phase II combined
facts.size: Located on approximately 150 hectares in the Beluluane Industrial Park
facts.jobs: Approximately 1,200 direct employees and several thousand contractor and indirect positions
facts.location: Beluluane Industrial Park, approximately 17 km west of Maputo, Mozambique
facts.status: Fully operational near nameplate capacity since 2003
facts.owner: South32 (47.1%), Mitsubishi (25%), IDC of South Africa (24%), Government of Mozambique (3.9%)

projects.name: OCP Jorf Lasfar Fertiliser Complex
projects.tagline: The world's largest phosphate fertiliser processing hub — anchoring Morocco's global leadership.
projects.overview: The OCP Jorf Lasfar complex is the world's largest integrated phosphate fertiliser production platform, located on the Atlantic coast near El Jadida, Morocco. Operated by OCP Group (Office Chérifien des Phosphates), the site produces phosphoric acid, diammonium phosphate (DAP), triple superphosphate (TSP), and NPK compound fertilisers. Morocco holds approximately 70% of the world's proven phosphate reserves, and OCP is the world's largest phosphate exporter. The Jorf Lasfar complex, combined with the OCP Group's mining operations at Khouribga, forms the backbone of Morocco's phosphate value chain, generating billions in export revenue annually.
objectives: Produce over 10 million tonnes per year of phosphate fertiliser products for global export markets
Maximise value addition from Morocco's phosphate rock reserves through downstream chemical processing
Supply affordable fertiliser to African farmers through the OCP Africa subsidiary initiative
Achieve environmental leadership in phosphate processing through desalination, solar power, and waste recovery
Maintain Morocco's position as the world's largest phosphate fertiliser exporter
Develop innovative customised fertiliser blends tailored to specific soil conditions across Africa
technologies: Wet-process phosphoric acid production using sulfuric acid digestion of phosphate rock
Granulation plants for producing DAP, MAP, TSP, and customised NPK fertiliser blends
Sulfuric acid production plants with waste-heat recovery for steam and power generation
Slurry pipeline system transporting phosphate rock 235 km from Khouribga mines to Jorf Lasfar
Seawater desalination plants providing process water, reducing freshwater consumption
Solar energy installations as part of OCP's green energy transition programme

milestones.title: Jorf Lasfar Complex Inaugurated
milestones.description: The first phosphoric acid and fertiliser production units at Jorf Lasfar commence operations, establishing a new centre of gravity for Morocco's phosphate processing industry.

milestones.title: OCP Corporatisation and Investment Programme
milestones.description: OCP transforms from a state enterprise into a limited company (S.A.) and launches a massive $20 billion investment programme to expand processing capacity and modernise operations.

milestones.title: Slurry Pipeline Operational
milestones.description: The 235 km phosphate slurry pipeline from Khouribga to Jorf Lasfar becomes fully operational, replacing truck and rail transport and reducing costs and emissions by an estimated 90%.

milestones.title: OCP Africa Subsidiary Launch
milestones.description: OCP establishes OCP Africa to promote tailored fertiliser solutions for sub-Saharan African markets, including soil mapping and customised NPK blends for specific crops and regions.

milestones.title: Green Energy and Desalination Expansion
milestones.description: OCP commissions new solar installations and desalination capacity at Jorf Lasfar as part of its sustainability strategy to halve carbon emissions and achieve water self-sufficiency.

milestones.title: Capacity Expansion to 2030 Targets
milestones.description: Ongoing expansion programmes targeting 20 million tonnes of total fertiliser capacity by 2030 across all OCP processing sites, with Jorf Lasfar as the primary hub.

resources.name: Phosphate Rock
resources.detail: Sourced from OCP's mines at Khouribga, 235 km inland, transported as slurry via dedicated pipeline. Morocco holds approximately 70% of global phosphate reserves.

resources.name: Sulfuric Acid Plants
resources.detail: Multiple sulfuric acid lines producing acid for phosphoric acid wet-process digestion. Waste heat is recovered for steam and electricity generation.

resources.name: Port Facilities
resources.detail: Dedicated port at Jorf Lasfar with bulk loading terminals for fertiliser export. Handles tens of millions of tonnes of product annually.

resources.name: Desalination Plant
resources.detail: Seawater desalination provides industrial process water, reducing OCP's freshwater consumption and ensuring operational resilience.

resources.name: Solar Energy
resources.detail: Growing solar photovoltaic capacity as part of OCP's target to source majority of energy from renewable sources by 2030.

projects.impact: OCP Group is the world's largest phosphate fertiliser exporter, with the Jorf Lasfar complex serving as its primary processing hub. The group contributes approximately 5% of Morocco's GDP and is the country's largest employer with over 20,000 direct employees. Through OCP Africa, the company has provided soil-specific fertiliser solutions to over 3 million smallholder farmers across sub-Saharan Africa, directly improving crop yields and food security. The slurry pipeline alone saves over 3 million tonnes of CO2 emissions annually compared to truck transport.
projects.videoTitle: OCP Jorf Lasfar — Feeding the World from Morocco

facts.capacity: Over 10 million tonnes per year of phosphate fertiliser products
facts.investment: Part of OCP's $20 billion investment programme launched in 2008
facts.size: Jorf Lasfar industrial platform spans over 1,800 hectares on the Atlantic coast
facts.jobs: OCP Group employs over 20,000 people directly with thousands more in contractors and services
facts.location: Jorf Lasfar, approximately 20 km south of El Jadida, Atlantic coast of Morocco
facts.status: Fully operational with ongoing expansion towards 2030 capacity targets
facts.owner: OCP Group S.A. — 95% owned by the Kingdom of Morocco

projects.name: Ethiopian Airlines MRO and Aviation Academy
projects.tagline: Africa's largest aviation maintenance facility and training powerhouse — keeping the continent flying.
projects.overview: Ethiopian Airlines Group operates Africa's largest and most advanced Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) facility, along with a world-class Aviation Academy, both based at Bole International Airport in Addis Ababa. The MRO facility can handle wide-body aircraft including the Boeing 787 Dreamliner and Airbus A350, providing heavy maintenance checks, engine overhaul, component repair, and aircraft painting for Ethiopian Airlines and third-party African and international carriers. The Ethiopian Aviation Academy is the largest aviation training institution in Africa, training over 4,000 students per year in pilot training, aircraft maintenance, cabin crew, and aviation management.
objectives: Provide full-spectrum MRO services for Ethiopian Airlines' fleet and third-party carriers across Africa
Reduce the need for African airlines to send aircraft overseas for heavy maintenance and overhaul
Train the next generation of African aviation professionals through the Aviation Academy
Generate foreign exchange revenue by attracting international customers for MRO services
Support Ethiopian Airlines' Vision 2035 strategy to become Africa's leading aviation group
Develop engine overhaul capability to service CFM56, GE90, and GEnx engine families
technologies: Wide-body aircraft maintenance hangars with capacity for Boeing 777, 787, and Airbus A350 heavy checks
Engine test cell and overhaul shop for CFM56 and GEnx turbofan engine families
Non-destructive testing (NDT) laboratory with ultrasonic, eddy current, and radiographic inspection capabilities
Full-flight simulators for Boeing 737 MAX, 787, 777, and Airbus A350 pilot training
Composite material repair facility for carbon-fibre reinforced polymer aircraft structures
Aircraft painting hangar with environmentally controlled spray booths for wide-body aircraft

milestones.title: Ethiopian Aviation Academy Founded
milestones.description: Ethiopian Airlines establishes its aviation training school, one of the first in Africa, initially offering pilot and technician training with support from TWA.

milestones.title: MRO Facility Major Expansion
milestones.description: Ethiopian Airlines opens expanded MRO hangars capable of handling wide-body aircraft. The facility begins attracting third-party maintenance contracts from other African carriers.

milestones.title: Boeing 787 Heavy Maintenance Capability
milestones.description: Ethiopian MRO achieves certification for Boeing 787 Dreamliner heavy maintenance, becoming the first African facility to offer this service for the composite-fuselage aircraft.

milestones.title: New Aviation Academy Campus
milestones.description: The new Aviation Academy campus opens with expanded capacity for 4,000 students annually. Facilities include new flight simulator bays and a dedicated cabin crew training centre.

milestones.title: Airbus A350 MRO Certification
milestones.description: Ethiopian MRO obtains certification for Airbus A350 base maintenance, making it a full-spectrum wide-body MRO provider for both Boeing and Airbus types.

milestones.title: MRO City at New Mega Airport
milestones.description: Planning and initial construction of a new MRO city at the planned Addis Ababa mega airport, targeting capacity to service 300 aircraft per year by 2035.

resources.name: Wide-Body Maintenance Hangars
resources.detail: Multiple hangars capable of accommodating Boeing 777, 787, and Airbus A350 aircraft for C-checks and D-checks (heavy maintenance).

resources.name: Engine Overhaul Shop
resources.detail: Full engine strip, repair, and reassembly facility with test cell for CFM56 and GEnx turbofan engines.

resources.name: Component Repair Shops
resources.detail: Dedicated workshops for avionics, hydraulics, pneumatics, landing gear, and composite structure repair.

resources.name: Full-Flight Simulators
resources.detail: Multiple Level D full-flight simulators for Boeing 737, 787, 777, and Airbus A350 type rating and recurrent training.

resources.name: Skilled Workforce
resources.detail: Over 4,000 MRO technicians and engineers, predominantly Ethiopian nationals, many trained at the in-house Aviation Academy.

projects.impact: Ethiopian Airlines' MRO division services over 200 aircraft per year from airlines across Africa, the Middle East, and Europe, generating hundreds of millions in foreign exchange for Ethiopia. The Aviation Academy has trained over 20,000 aviation professionals from 40+ African countries, making it the primary source of pilot and technician talent on the continent. The combined MRO and Academy operation has positioned Addis Ababa as Africa's aviation capital and supports Ethiopian Airlines' status as Africa's largest and most profitable airline.

facts.jobs: Over 4,000 MRO technicians and engineers plus 4,000 Academy students per year

facts.status: Fully operational with ongoing expansion as part of Vision 2035

projects.name: Volkswagen South Africa Uitenhage Plant
projects.tagline: Africa's most advanced automotive manufacturing plant — producing the Polo for the world.
projects.overview: Volkswagen South Africa (VWSA) operates one of Africa's most advanced automotive manufacturing plants in Kariega (formerly Uitenhage), Eastern Cape. Established in 1946, the plant has evolved into a major global export hub, producing the Volkswagen Polo and Polo Vivo for both domestic and international markets. The facility includes a full body shop, paint shop, and assembly line, and was selected as the sole producer of the right-hand-drive Polo for global markets. Under South Africa's Automotive Production and Development Programme (APDP), VWSA has invested billions of rand in modernising the plant, which exports approximately 70% of its production to over 30 countries.
objectives: Manufacture over 160,000 vehicles per year for domestic sale and export to global markets
Serve as the global production hub for right-hand-drive Volkswagen Polo models
Maximise local content and develop the Eastern Cape automotive supplier ecosystem
Contribute to South Africa's automotive sector which targets 1% of global production by 2035
Maintain world-class quality standards matching VW Group's global benchmarks
Train and employ thousands of South Africans in advanced manufacturing
technologies: Automated body shop with over 600 robots for welding, sealing, and material handling
Advanced paint shop with cathodic dip coating, primer surfacer, basecoat, and clearcoat application
Fully integrated assembly line with just-in-sequence parts delivery from local and international suppliers
Laser quality measurement systems for body-in-white dimensional accuracy verification
Water-based paint technology and regenerative thermal oxidisers for environmental compliance
Enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems integrated with VW Group's global production network

milestones.title: Plant Established
milestones.description: South African Motor Assemblers and Distributors (SAMAD) begins assembling Volkswagen vehicles in Uitenhage, marking the start of VW manufacturing in Africa.

milestones.title: Citi Golf Success
milestones.description: The plant's Citi Golf becomes South Africa's best-selling vehicle, demonstrating the market strength of locally produced affordable cars.

milestones.title: New Polo Production Launch
milestones.description: VWSA launches production of the new-generation Polo and Polo Vivo, marking a major investment in retooling. The plant becomes a key export hub for right-hand-drive markets.

milestones.title: R6.5 Billion Investment Announced
milestones.description: VWSA announces a R6.5 billion investment to upgrade the plant for production of the latest Polo model, including a new paint shop and body shop modernisation.

milestones.title: Millionth Polo Exported
milestones.description: The plant celebrates exporting its millionth Polo, cementing its status as one of VW Group's key global production sites for the model.

milestones.title: Next-Generation Model Preparation
milestones.description: VWSA prepares for the transition to next-generation models with ongoing investment in plant modernisation and supplier development.

resources.name: Body Shop
resources.detail: Highly automated body shop with over 600 robots performing spot welding, MIG/MAG welding, adhesive bonding, and component assembly.

resources.name: Paint Shop
resources.detail: State-of-the-art paint facility applying cathodic dip coating, primer, basecoat, and clearcoat. Uses water-based paints for environmental compliance.

resources.name: Assembly Hall
resources.detail: Final assembly line integrating powertrain, interior, chassis, and electrical systems. Produces over 700 vehicles per day at peak.

resources.name: Supplier Park
resources.detail: Cluster of Tier 1 suppliers located near the plant providing seats, dashboards, exhaust systems, and other components on a just-in-sequence basis.

resources.name: Port Elizabeth Harbour
resources.detail: Vehicles for export are shipped through nearby Gqeberha (Port Elizabeth) harbour to over 30 countries worldwide.

projects.impact: VWSA is the largest private sector employer in the Eastern Cape, directly employing over 4,000 people with approximately 11,000 indirect jobs in the supplier network. The plant contributes over R30 billion annually to South Africa's manufacturing GDP and accounts for a significant share of the country's automotive exports. VWSA has been instrumental in developing the Nelson Mandela Bay region as one of Africa's premier automotive manufacturing hubs alongside BMW, Ford, and General Motors operations in the area.

projects.name: Indorama Eleme Petrochemicals
projects.tagline: West Africa's only integrated petrochemical and fertiliser complex — from gas to granules.
projects.overview: Indorama Eleme Petrochemicals Limited (IEPL) is an integrated olefins and polyolefins complex located in the Eleme Petrochemical Industrial Zone in Rivers State, Nigeria. Originally built by the Nigerian government in the 1990s and privatised in 2006 when Indorama Corporation acquired a majority stake, the complex has been transformed through over $3 billion in investment. The facility produces polyethylene and polypropylene, and Indorama later added a world-scale fertiliser plant (Indorama Eleme Fertiliser & Chemicals) producing 1.5 million MTPA of urea. The fertiliser plant, commissioned in 2016, was the largest private-sector investment in Nigeria at the time.
objectives: Produce 240,000 tonnes per year of polyethylene and polypropylene for Nigerian and West African markets
Produce 1.5 million tonnes per year of granulated urea for domestic and export fertiliser markets
Monetise Nigerian natural gas through conversion to high-value petrochemical and fertiliser products
Substitute petrochemical imports and reduce Nigeria's foreign exchange expenditure on plastic raw materials
Create thousands of jobs in manufacturing, services, and downstream plastics conversion industries
Support Nigerian agriculture by providing affordable domestically-produced fertiliser
technologies: Gas-phase polyethylene production using fluidised bed reactor technology
Polypropylene production using Spheripol loop reactor process
Ammonia synthesis and urea granulation for fertiliser production using Haldor Topsoe technology
Natural gas processing and ethylene cracking for feedstock preparation
Advanced distributed control systems (DCS) for integrated process monitoring
Effluent treatment and waste management systems meeting international environmental standards

milestones.title: Original Complex Commissioned
milestones.description: The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) commissions the Eleme Petrochemicals Company with an olefins cracker and polyethylene plant.

milestones.title: Privatisation — Indorama Acquisition
milestones.description: Indorama Corporation of Thailand/Indonesia acquires a 75% stake in the moribund complex and begins a comprehensive rehabilitation and expansion programme.

milestones.title: Petrochemical Plant Revitalised
milestones.description: Following extensive turnaround and debottlenecking, the petrochemical complex reaches full production. Polypropylene production line added to the existing polyethylene capacity.

milestones.title: Fertiliser Plant Commissioned
milestones.description: The $1.5 billion Indorama Eleme Fertiliser & Chemicals plant is commissioned, producing 1.5 million MTPA of urea. It is the first new-build fertiliser plant in Nigeria in over 25 years.

milestones.title: Train 2 Fertiliser Plant Operational
milestones.description: A second fertiliser train comes online, further increasing Indorama's urea production capacity at the Eleme complex.

milestones.title: Methanol and Derivatives Expansion
milestones.description: Indorama progresses plans for a methanol plant and further downstream chemical derivatives to expand the integrated complex.

resources.name: Natural Gas Feedstock
resources.detail: Gas supplied from the Niger Delta gas fields. The complex consumes large volumes of gas as both feedstock (for ethylene cracking and ammonia synthesis) and fuel.

resources.name: Olefins Cracker
resources.detail: Gas cracker producing ethylene and propylene as primary feedstocks for polyethylene and polypropylene polymerisation.

resources.name: Fertiliser Plant
resources.detail: Ammonia-urea complex producing 1.5 million MTPA of granulated urea for agricultural use.

resources.name: Port Harcourt Port Access
resources.detail: Proximity to Port Harcourt and Onne port facilities for both feedstock import and product export.

resources.name: Industrial Free Zone
resources.detail: Located in the Eleme Petrochemical Industrial Zone with supporting infrastructure including power, water, and transport.

projects.impact: Indorama's transformation of the Eleme complex from a moribund state-owned facility to a thriving $3 billion petrochemical and fertiliser hub is one of Africa's most successful privatisation stories. The complex directly employs over 2,500 people and supports thousands of downstream jobs in plastics manufacturing and agriculture. The fertiliser plant alone saves Nigeria an estimated $500 million per year in import costs and provides affordable urea to millions of farmers. Indorama is the largest Nigerian company in terms of foreign direct investment from Southeast Asia.

facts.capacity: 240,000 MTPA polyethylene/polypropylene plus 1.5 million MTPA urea fertiliser
facts.investment: Over $3 billion total investment since Indorama's 2006 acquisition
facts.size: Integrated complex in the Eleme Petrochemical Industrial Zone covering hundreds of hectares
facts.jobs: Over 2,500 direct employees and thousands of indirect jobs
facts.location: Eleme Petrochemical Industrial Zone, Rivers State, Nigeria
facts.status: Fully operational petrochemicals and fertiliser with further expansion underway
facts.owner: Indorama Corporation (Thailand/Indonesia) — 75% stake, Nigerian partners 25%

projects.name: Lafarge Africa Cement Operations
projects.tagline: One of Africa's largest cement producers — building the continent from Ewekoro to Mfamosing.
projects.region: Ewekoro & Sagamu (Ogun State), Mfamosing (Cross River State)
projects.overview: Lafarge Africa Plc, a subsidiary of the global Holcim Group, operates major cement manufacturing plants across Nigeria including facilities at Ewekoro and Sagamu in Ogun State, and Mfamosing in Cross River State. The company has a combined installed capacity of approximately 10.5 million tonnes per annum (MTPA) of cement, making it one of Nigeria's top three cement producers. Lafarge Africa also operates a significant presence in South Africa through its aggregates and ready-mix businesses. The Mfamosing plant in Cross River State is one of the most modern cement plants in West Africa, featuring dry-process technology and a captive limestone quarry.
objectives: Produce over 10 million tonnes per year of cement to meet Nigeria's growing infrastructure demand
Supply high-quality cement products including Ordinary Portland Cement, Rapid Hardening, and Oilwell cement
Operate sustainable cement manufacturing with increasing use of alternative fuels and raw materials
Support Nigeria's housing deficit reduction by providing affordable building materials
Maintain market leadership in Nigeria's cement sector alongside Dangote Cement and BUA Cement
technologies: Dry-process rotary kiln technology with preheater and precalciner systems for energy-efficient clinker production
Vertical roller mills for raw material grinding and cement finishing achieving high fineness
Alternative fuels and raw materials (AFR) co-processing system for waste-derived fuel utilisation
Automated quality control laboratories with X-ray fluorescence (XRF) analysers for real-time product monitoring
Dust collection and electrostatic precipitator systems for particulate emission control
SAP-integrated enterprise management and logistics systems for production and distribution optimisation

milestones.title: Ewekoro Plant Established
milestones.description: The West African Portland Cement Company establishes cement manufacturing at Ewekoro in Ogun State, one of the earliest large-scale cement plants in Nigeria.

milestones.title: Lafarge Acquires Majority Stake
milestones.description: The French multinational Lafarge acquires controlling interest, bringing global best practices in cement manufacturing and investing in plant modernisation.

milestones.title: Mfamosing Plant Commissioned
milestones.description: The modern greenfield Mfamosing plant in Cross River State is commissioned with 2.5 MTPA capacity, using state-of-the-art dry-process technology.

milestones.title: LafargeHolcim Merger
milestones.description: Lafarge and Holcim merge globally. The Nigerian operations continue as Lafarge Africa under the Holcim Group, with enhanced access to global technology and capital.

milestones.title: Sustainability and AFR Programme
milestones.description: Lafarge Africa expands its alternative fuels programme, co-processing waste materials in cement kilns to reduce fossil fuel consumption and manage industrial waste.

milestones.title: Capacity Optimisation
milestones.description: Ongoing debottlenecking and optimisation programmes across all plants to maximise throughput and improve energy efficiency per tonne of cement.

resources.name: Limestone Quarries
resources.detail: Captive limestone quarries at each plant location providing the primary raw material for clinker production.

resources.name: Rotary Kilns
resources.detail: Multiple dry-process kilns with preheater towers across three plant locations producing Portland cement clinker.

resources.name: Grinding Mills
resources.detail: Vertical roller mills and ball mills for raw material preparation and final cement grinding.

resources.name: Distribution Fleet
resources.detail: Extensive truck distribution network and bulk cement terminals for product delivery across Nigeria.

resources.name: Quality Laboratories
resources.detail: Automated labs at each plant performing continuous testing of raw materials, clinker, and finished cement.

projects.impact: Lafarge Africa supplies cement to some of Nigeria's most important infrastructure projects, from roads and bridges to housing developments and commercial buildings. The company directly employs over 3,000 people and supports tens of thousands of jobs in transport, distribution, and construction. Through its alternative fuels programme, Lafarge Africa processes thousands of tonnes of waste annually that would otherwise go to landfill, contributing to circular economy goals. The company's presence ensures competitive cement pricing in a market serving over 200 million Nigerians.

facts.jobs: Over 3,000 direct employees plus thousands in logistics and distribution

facts.status: Fully operational across all plants with ongoing optimisation

projects.name: Helios Towers Pan-African Telecom Infrastructure
projects.tagline: Africa's leading independent tower company — connecting millions through shared infrastructure.
projects.region: Tanzania, DRC, Congo, Ghana, South Africa, Senegal, Madagascar, Malawi, Oman
projects.overview: Helios Towers is one of Africa's largest independent telecommunications tower companies, operating over 14,000 tower sites across 9 markets in Africa and the Middle East. Founded in 2010 and listed on the London Stock Exchange in 2019, the company builds, acquires, and operates passive telecommunications infrastructure — towers, power systems, and shelters — that mobile network operators (MNOs) lease to deploy their radio equipment. By sharing towers among multiple operators, Helios Towers reduces the cost of network rollout and enables faster expansion of mobile connectivity across underserved areas. The company is a critical enabler of Africa's mobile revolution.
objectives: Operate and expand a portfolio of over 14,000 telecommunications tower sites across Africa
Enable mobile network operators to expand coverage efficiently through shared infrastructure
Increase tenancy ratios by hosting multiple operators on each tower to maximise asset utilisation
Reduce carbon emissions by transitioning tower power systems from diesel to solar and hybrid solutions
Bridge the digital divide by extending telecom infrastructure into rural and underserved communities
Deliver attractive returns to investors while supporting Africa's digital transformation
technologies: Lattice steel and monopole tower designs ranging from 25 to 60+ metres for urban and rural coverage
Hybrid power systems combining solar panels, lithium-ion batteries, and backup diesel generators
Remote monitoring and management (RMM) systems for real-time tower performance tracking
Site sharing technology enabling multiple MNOs to co-locate equipment on a single tower structure
Fibre backhaul integration at key tower sites for high-capacity data transmission
Smart energy management systems to optimise power consumption and minimise diesel usage

milestones.title: Helios Towers Founded
milestones.description: Helios Towers is established to build and operate shared telecommunications infrastructure across Africa, initially focusing on the Democratic Republic of Congo and Tanzania.

milestones.title: Portfolio Reaches 6,000 Towers

milestones.title: London Stock Exchange IPO
milestones.description: Helios Towers lists on the London Stock Exchange with a market capitalisation exceeding $1.5 billion, raising capital for further expansion across Africa.

milestones.title: Expansion into New Markets
milestones.description: Helios enters Senegal, Madagascar, and Oman through acquisitions, diversifying beyond its original East and West African markets.

milestones.title: South Africa Market Entry
milestones.description: Helios acquires tower assets in South Africa, entering Africa's largest and most developed telecom market for the first time.

milestones.title: 14,000+ Towers and Green Energy Push
milestones.description: Portfolio exceeds 14,000 towers. Helios accelerates its green energy programme, targeting 70% hybrid or solar-powered sites to reduce diesel dependency.

resources.name: Tower Portfolio
resources.detail: Over 14,000 telecommunications tower sites across 9 countries, including ground-based towers, rooftop installations, and in-building solutions.

resources.name: Power Systems
resources.detail: Hybrid power solutions combining solar panels, batteries, and diesel generators. Ongoing transition towards predominantly renewable energy.

resources.name: Field Operations Teams
resources.detail: Thousands of field technicians performing tower construction, maintenance, and power management across all operating markets.

resources.name: Network Operations Centre
resources.detail: Centralised and regional NOCs providing 24/7 remote monitoring of tower uptime, power status, and security alerts.

resources.name: Fibre and Backhaul Assets

projects.impact: Helios Towers directly enables mobile connectivity for hundreds of millions of Africans by providing the infrastructure that mobile operators need to extend their networks. The shared tower model reduces the cost of network deployment by an estimated 30-50% compared to each operator building its own towers. The company employs over 2,000 people across its markets and supports Africa's digital economy growth. By transitioning from diesel to solar-hybrid power, Helios is eliminating thousands of tonnes of CO2 emissions annually from Africa's telecom infrastructure.

projects.name: Sasol Secunda Synfuels Complex
projects.tagline: The world's largest coal-to-liquids plant — South Africa's strategic fuel independence engine.
projects.overview: The Sasol Secunda complex in Mpumalanga Province is the world's largest commercial coal-to-liquids (CTL) facility, converting coal into synthetic fuels and chemicals using proprietary Fischer-Tropsch technology. With a production capacity of approximately 160,000 barrels per day of synthetic fuels and chemicals, Secunda supplies approximately 28% of South Africa's liquid fuel requirements. The complex also produces a wide range of chemical products including ethylene, propylene, ammonia, solvents, and waxes. Built during the apartheid era to ensure fuel self-sufficiency, Secunda remains one of the most complex industrial operations in the world and is Sasol's primary production asset.
objectives: Produce approximately 160,000 barrels per day of synthetic fuels and chemicals from coal feedstock
Supply approximately 28% of South Africa's total liquid fuel demand from domestic coal resources
Manufacture a broad range of commodity and speciality chemicals for domestic and export markets
Maintain South Africa's strategic fuel production capability from indigenous energy resources
Reduce the carbon intensity of operations through the Future Sasol programme and energy transition initiatives
Sustain Secunda's role as the economic anchor of the Mpumalanga Highveld region
technologies: Sasol proprietary Fischer-Tropsch synthesis converting syngas into liquid hydrocarbons
Lurgi fixed-bed dry-bottom gasification technology converting coal to synthesis gas (syngas)
Over 80 Sasol Advanced Synthol (SAS) circulating fluidised bed reactors for FT synthesis
Extensive downstream refining and chemical separation trains for product purification
Oxygen production via multiple air separation units (ASUs) — among the largest globally
Water treatment and recycling systems processing millions of litres daily

milestones.title: Sasol Two Commissioned
milestones.description: The first of the two Secunda synfuels plants (Sasol Two) is commissioned, built at massive scale and speed in response to the global oil crisis and apartheid-era sanctions.

milestones.title: Sasol Three Commissioned
milestones.description: The second plant (Sasol Three) comes online, doubling the complex's capacity. Combined, the two plants make Secunda the world's largest synthetic fuels facility.

milestones.title: Reactor Upgrade Programme
milestones.description: Sasol completes its Sasol Advanced Synthol (SAS) reactor upgrade programme, replacing the older Synthol reactors with more efficient circulating fluidised bed technology.

milestones.title: Growth in Chemicals Portfolio
milestones.description: Secunda expands its chemicals production portfolio, increasingly shifting value towards higher-margin chemical products alongside fuel production.

milestones.title: Future Sasol Decarbonisation Plan
milestones.description: Sasol launches its Future Sasol roadmap targeting a 30% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 from its 2017 baseline, including plans for renewable energy and green hydrogen.

milestones.title: Energy Transition Investments
milestones.description: Sasol progresses green hydrogen pilot projects and solar/wind power procurement to reduce coal dependency at Secunda over the long term.

resources.name: Coal Supply
resources.detail: Approximately 40 million tonnes per year of coal from Sasol Mining's captive collieries and external suppliers on the Mpumalanga Highveld.

resources.name: Gasifiers
resources.detail: Over 80 Lurgi gasifiers converting coal into synthesis gas using steam and oxygen at high temperature and pressure.

resources.name: Fischer-Tropsch Reactors
resources.detail: Multiple Sasol Advanced Synthol (SAS) reactors converting syngas into synthetic crude and chemical intermediates.

resources.name: Air Separation Units
resources.detail: Multiple large-scale air separation units producing industrial quantities of oxygen for coal gasification.

resources.name: Water Infrastructure
resources.detail: Large water allocation from the Vaal River system, extensive recycling and treatment facilities to minimise freshwater consumption.

projects.impact: Secunda is the largest single-site employer in Mpumalanga Province, directly employing over 8,000 Sasol staff and approximately 15,000 contractors. The complex generates tens of billions of rand in annual revenue and contributes significantly to South Africa's GDP. It provides strategic fuel security by producing approximately 7.3 billion litres of fuel annually from domestic coal, reducing reliance on imported crude oil. However, Secunda is also one of the world's largest single-point sources of greenhouse gas emissions, making its decarbonisation a critical challenge for South Africa's climate commitments.
projects.videoTitle: Sasol Secunda — Coal to Chemicals at Massive Scale

projects.name: BUA Cement Production Complex
projects.tagline: Nigeria's fastest-growing cement company — from Sokoto limestone to national champion.
projects.region: Sokoto State, Edo State, Adamawa State
projects.overview: BUA Cement Plc is Nigeria's second-largest cement producer with an installed capacity of approximately 17 million tonnes per annum (MTPA) across multiple plants. The company's flagship operations are in Sokoto State (Kalambaina plant) and Edo State (Okpella plant), with additional capacity in Adamawa State. BUA Cement was formed in January 2020 through the merger of the Cement Company of Northern Nigeria (CCNN) and BUA's Obu Cement Company. The company is owned by Abdul Samad Rabiu, one of Africa's wealthiest industrialists, and is listed on the Nigerian Stock Exchange with a market capitalisation of over N3 trillion.
objectives: Achieve and maintain installed cement production capacity exceeding 17 million MTPA
Challenge Dangote Cement's market dominance in Nigeria by offering competitive pricing
Expand distribution networks across all six geopolitical zones of Nigeria
Develop export markets in neighbouring West African countries
Integrate backward into limestone mining and power generation for cost efficiency
Contribute to Nigeria's cement self-sufficiency and potential export surplus
technologies: Dry-process rotary kiln systems with five-stage preheater and precalciner for energy-efficient clinker production
Vertical roller mills for raw material and cement grinding with low specific energy consumption
Captive power plants at major production sites to ensure uninterrupted energy supply
Automated process control using ABB and Siemens DCS systems for production optimisation
Bag filter and electrostatic precipitator systems for dust emission control
GPS-enabled fleet management for distribution logistics across Nigeria

milestones.title: Obu Cement Line 1 Commissioned
milestones.description: BUA commissions the first line of the Obu Cement plant in Edo State with 1.5 MTPA capacity, marking BUA's entry into integrated cement manufacturing.

milestones.title: Obu Line 2 and Sokoto Expansion
milestones.description: BUA doubles Obu capacity with a second kiln line and expands the Sokoto (CCNN) plant, bringing combined capacity to over 8 MTPA.

milestones.title: BUA Cement Merger and NSE Listing
milestones.description: The merger of CCNN and Obu Cement creates BUA Cement Plc, listed on the Nigerian Stock Exchange. Becomes Nigeria's second-largest cement company by capacity.

milestones.title: Sokoto Line 4 Commissioned
milestones.description: A new 3 MTPA production line is commissioned at the Kalambaina plant in Sokoto, significantly boosting BUA Cement's northern Nigeria production capacity.

milestones.title: Adamawa Plant Begins Production
milestones.description: BUA commissions a new cement plant in Adamawa State, northeast Nigeria, expanding its geographic footprint into an underserved region.

milestones.title: Capacity Target of 20 MTPA
milestones.description: BUA continues expansion with a target of reaching 20 million MTPA of installed capacity through new lines at existing plants and potential greenfield developments.

resources.name: Limestone Reserves
resources.detail: Extensive captive limestone deposits at Sokoto, Edo, and Adamawa plant sites with reserves estimated at hundreds of millions of tonnes.

resources.name: Cement Kilns
resources.detail: Multiple rotary kiln lines across three plant locations producing Portland cement clinker using dry-process technology.

resources.name: Captive Power Plants
resources.detail: Gas-fired and dual-fuel power generation at plant sites to ensure reliable electricity supply independent of the national grid.

resources.name: Distribution Network
resources.detail: Nationwide truck fleet and depot system for distributing bagged and bulk cement across Nigeria's six geopolitical zones.

resources.name: Packaging Plants
resources.detail: High-speed rotary packing machines at each plant producing 50 kg bags for the retail market.

projects.impact: BUA Cement has intensified competition in Nigeria's cement sector, contributing to more stable cement prices for consumers and construction companies. The company directly employs over 5,000 people and supports tens of thousands of indirect jobs in transport, distribution, and construction. BUA Cement's rapid growth from a single plant to a 17 MTPA producer in under a decade demonstrates the potential for large-scale industrial development in Nigeria. The Sokoto operations have catalysed economic development in Nigeria's northwest, one of the country's most economically challenged regions.

facts.capacity: Approximately 17 million tonnes per annum installed capacity across three states
facts.investment: Over $2 billion invested in cement plant construction and expansion since 2014
facts.size: Major plant locations in Sokoto State, Edo State, and Adamawa State
facts.jobs: Over 5,000 direct employees and tens of thousands of indirect positions
facts.location: Kalambaina (Sokoto), Okpella/Obu (Edo), and Adamawa State, Nigeria

facts.owner: BUA Cement Plc — majority owned by Abdul Samad Rabiu's BUA Group, listed on NGX

projects.name: Zipline Drone Delivery Network
projects.tagline: The world's first national drone delivery service — saving lives across Africa with autonomous logistics.
projects.overview: Zipline operates the world's first and largest autonomous drone delivery network for medical supplies, initially launched in Rwanda in 2016 and expanded to Ghana in 2019. Using custom-designed fixed-wing drones capable of carrying 1.8 kg payloads over a range of 160 km round trip, Zipline delivers blood products, vaccines, medications, and other essential medical supplies to hospitals and health centres across the country within 30 minutes of an order. The company operates from distribution centres that can each serve an area of approximately 22,000 square kilometres. Zipline has completed over 1 million commercial deliveries across its operations, making it the world's most experienced drone delivery operator.
objectives: Deliver essential medical supplies to every hospital and health centre in Rwanda and Ghana within 30 minutes
Eliminate stockouts of critical blood products and medicines at remote health facilities
Reduce medical waste by enabling on-demand delivery instead of overstocking at peripheral facilities
Demonstrate the viability of drone logistics for healthcare in developing countries
Expand autonomous delivery infrastructure to additional African countries and use cases
Complete millions of deliveries per year, making drone logistics routine infrastructure
technologies: Custom fixed-wing autonomous drones (Zip) with 1.8 kg payload and 160 km round-trip range
Catapult launch system requiring no runway — drones are accelerated to flight speed in under a second
Precision parachute delivery system dropping packages into a target zone the size of two parking spaces
Automated order management system integrated with hospital SMS and WhatsApp ordering
GPS navigation with beyond-visual-line-of-sight (BVLOS) operations under national aviation authority approval
Next-generation Platform 2 (P2) drone capable of serving a wider range of last-mile delivery needs

milestones.title: Rwanda National Service Launch
milestones.description: Zipline launches the world's first national drone delivery service from its Muhanga distribution centre in Rwanda, initially delivering blood products to 21 hospitals.

milestones.title: Ghana Operations Begin
milestones.description: Zipline expands to Ghana with four distribution centres, delivering blood, vaccines, and essential medications to over 2,000 health facilities across the country.

milestones.title: COVID-19 Response Deliveries
milestones.description: Zipline delivers COVID-19 test samples, PPE, and later vaccines across Rwanda and Ghana, demonstrating drone logistics as critical pandemic response infrastructure.

milestones.title: One Million Deliveries Milestone
milestones.description: Zipline surpasses one million commercial deliveries globally, with the majority in Rwanda and Ghana. Delivery reliability exceeds 99%.

milestones.title: Platform 2 Drone Launch
milestones.description: Zipline unveils its next-generation Platform 2 autonomous delivery system, designed for both rural long-range and urban short-range delivery applications.

milestones.title: Further African Expansion
milestones.description: Zipline explores expansion to additional African markets while scaling operations in Rwanda and Ghana to cover broader categories of medical and commercial deliveries.

resources.name: Distribution Centres
resources.detail: Purpose-built facilities housing drone launch pads, inventory storage, and automated loading systems. Each centre can serve a radius of approximately 80 km.

resources.name: Drone Fleet
resources.detail: Fleet of custom-built fixed-wing Zip drones operating multiple flights per day from each distribution centre. Cruising speed of approximately 110 km/h.

resources.name: Medical Inventory
resources.detail: Comprehensive stock of blood products (all types), vaccines (maintained in cold chain), essential medications, and medical supplies at each distribution centre.

resources.name: Operations Team
resources.detail: Locally hired and trained operators, maintenance technicians, and logistics coordinators at each distribution centre.

resources.name: Aviation Partnerships
resources.detail: Formal agreements with Rwanda Civil Aviation Authority and Ghana Civil Aviation Authority for BVLOS commercial drone operations.

projects.impact: Zipline has fundamentally changed healthcare logistics in Rwanda and Ghana, ensuring that every health facility can access critical blood products, vaccines, and medications within 30 minutes regardless of distance or road conditions. The service has prevented countless deaths from postpartum haemorrhage and other conditions requiring urgent blood transfusion. In Rwanda alone, Zipline serves 12 million people — the entire population — with instant access to medical supplies. The success in Africa has made Zipline the blueprint for drone delivery systems worldwide and has attracted over $400 million in investment.
projects.videoTitle: Zipline — Drones Saving Lives in Africa

facts.capacity: Over 1 million deliveries completed; each centre handles hundreds of flights per day
facts.investment: Over $400 million raised in venture funding from Sequoia Capital, Google Ventures, and others
facts.size: Operations in Rwanda and Ghana covering national-scale delivery networks
facts.jobs: Hundreds of locally employed operators, technicians, and logistics staff across Africa

facts.status: Fully operational in Rwanda and Ghana with ongoing expansion

projects.name: M-PESA Mobile Money Infrastructure
projects.tagline: The mobile money revolution that banked the unbanked — Kenya's gift to global fintech.
projects.overview: M-PESA is the world's most successful mobile money platform, launched by Safaricom in Kenya in 2007. The service enables users to deposit, withdraw, transfer money, pay for goods and services, and access financial products using a basic mobile phone, without requiring a traditional bank account. As of 2024, M-PESA processes over $300 billion in annual transaction value across 7 African countries and serves over 60 million active users. The underlying infrastructure includes a network of over 600,000 agent outlets, sophisticated real-time transaction processing systems, and deep integration with Kenya's banking sector. M-PESA has become a case study in financial inclusion and is widely credited with lifting millions of Kenyans out of extreme poverty.
objectives: Provide accessible financial services to unbanked and underbanked populations across Africa
Process hundreds of billions of dollars in annual transaction value securely and reliably
Maintain a network of over 600,000 agent outlets as the physical cash-in/cash-out infrastructure
Expand the M-PESA ecosystem to include savings, lending, insurance, and investment products
Enable businesses of all sizes to accept digital payments and access working capital
Scale the platform across multiple African and international markets
technologies: Real-time transaction processing platform handling thousands of transactions per second
USSD and STK (SIM Toolkit) interface enabling mobile money on basic feature phones without internet
M-PESA Super App for smartphone users with enhanced features including QR payments and mini-apps
API gateway enabling third-party developers and businesses to integrate with M-PESA payments
Advanced fraud detection and anti-money laundering systems using machine learning algorithms
Agent management platform for onboarding, monitoring, and settling the 600,000+ agent network

milestones.title: M-PESA Launch in Kenya
milestones.description: Safaricom launches M-PESA as a person-to-person mobile money transfer service in Kenya. Within the first month, over 20,000 customers register.

milestones.title: M-Shwari Savings and Loans
milestones.description: M-PESA partners with Commercial Bank of Africa to launch M-Shwari, a mobile savings and micro-lending product that processes over 1 million loan applications in its first year.

milestones.title: Lipa Na M-PESA Merchant Payments
milestones.description: The Lipa Na M-PESA till number system enables hundreds of thousands of businesses to accept cashless payments, transforming retail commerce in Kenya.

milestones.title: COVID-19 Digital Acceleration
milestones.description: M-PESA transaction volumes surge as Kenya accelerates shift to digital payments during the pandemic. Safaricom waives transaction fees for small transfers to support social distancing.

milestones.title: M-PESA Super App and Global Expansion
milestones.description: M-PESA launches its Super App with enhanced features. Active users surpass 50 million across 7 countries. Annual transaction value exceeds $300 billion.

milestones.title: Financial Ecosystem Deepening
milestones.description: Continued expansion of M-PESA's financial services ecosystem including insurance, investment products, and cross-border remittances. Platform processes over 60 million transactions daily.

resources.name: Transaction Processing Platform
resources.detail: High-availability, real-time processing system handling over 60 million daily transactions with 99.99% uptime requirements.

resources.name: Agent Network
resources.detail: Over 600,000 registered M-PESA agents across Africa providing cash-in, cash-out, and customer registration services.

resources.name: Safaricom Mobile Network
resources.detail: Leverages Safaricom's mobile network infrastructure including base stations, transmission, and data centres for service delivery.

resources.name: Regulatory Partnerships
resources.detail: Licensed mobile money operations in partnership with central banks across 7 African countries including Kenya, Tanzania, and Ghana.

resources.name: M-PESA Africa Joint Venture
resources.detail: Joint venture between Vodacom (Vodafone subsidiary) and Safaricom managing M-PESA's pan-African expansion and technology development.

projects.impact: Research published in Science magazine found that M-PESA lifted approximately 194,000 Kenyan households (2% of the population) out of extreme poverty between 2008 and 2014. The platform processes over $300 billion annually — equivalent to more than three times Kenya's GDP — making it the backbone of the Kenyan economy. M-PESA has enabled financial inclusion for tens of millions of previously unbanked Africans and has spawned an entire ecosystem of fintech companies. Over 600,000 agent outlets provide employment across Africa, and the platform has become a model replicated across the developing world.

projects.name: Bamburi Cement (ARM Cement / Holcim East Africa)
projects.tagline: East Africa's largest cement manufacturer — six decades of building Kenya, Uganda, and beyond.
projects.region: Athi River & Mombasa, Kenya; Tororo, Uganda
projects.overview: Bamburi Cement Limited is East Africa's largest cement producer, operating plants in Kenya and Uganda with a combined installed capacity of approximately 6 million tonnes per annum. Originally established in 1951 at Bamburi near Mombasa, the company is now part of the Holcim Group following the acquisition of its former parent Lafarge. Key production sites include the Athi River plant near Nairobi, the historic Bamburi plant in Mombasa, and the Hima Cement plant in Tororo, Uganda. Bamburi produces a range of products including Portland cement, Pozzolanic cement, and speciality cements for road and dam construction. The company is listed on the Nairobi Securities Exchange.
objectives: Maintain leadership in the East African cement market with over 6 MTPA installed capacity
Serve the infrastructure boom across Kenya, Uganda, and the wider East African Community region
Advance sustainable cement production through alternative fuels and reduced clinker ratio
Develop innovative low-carbon cement products for the East African construction sector
Support Kenya's Vision 2030 and Uganda's infrastructure development plans with reliable supply
technologies: Dry-process rotary kiln systems with multi-stage preheaters at Athi River and Hima plants
Portland Pozzolana Cement (PPC) blending using volcanic pozzolana to reduce clinker content and CO2 emissions
Alternative fuels co-processing using industrial waste, biomass, and agricultural residues in kilns
Automated quality monitoring with on-line analysers for consistent product quality
Baobab Eco-Restoration Programme at the historic Bamburi quarry — a globally recognised rehabilitation project

milestones.title: Bamburi Cement Founded
milestones.description: Bamburi Portland Cement Company is established in Bamburi, Mombasa, making it one of the oldest cement manufacturers in East Africa.

milestones.title: Athi River Plant Commissioned
milestones.description: A major new plant at Athi River near Nairobi is commissioned, providing capacity closer to Kenya's main construction market and reducing transport costs.

milestones.title: ARM Cement Acquisition of Hima
milestones.description: The wider East African cement landscape consolidates as major players acquire and expand. Bamburi, through its parent, controls Hima Cement in Uganda.

milestones.title: Holcim Group Integration
milestones.description: Following the global LafargeHolcim merger, Bamburi Cement becomes part of the Holcim Group, gaining access to enhanced technology and global best practices.

milestones.title: Green Cement and AFR Expansion
milestones.description: Bamburi increases use of alternative fuels and raw materials, targeting 15%+ thermal substitution rate. Launches lower-carbon cement products for the Kenyan market.

milestones.title: Potential Ownership Transition
milestones.description: Holcim explores strategic options for its East African cement assets, with potential for new ownership or partnership that could reshape the regional market.

resources.name: Athi River Plant
resources.detail: Major production facility near Nairobi producing over 2 million tonnes per year of Portland and Pozzolanic cement for the Kenyan market.

resources.name: Bamburi Mombasa Plant
resources.detail: Historic plant on the Kenyan coast producing cement and serving the coastal and export markets. Adjacent to the famous rehabilitated quarry.

resources.name: Hima Cement (Tororo, Uganda)
resources.detail: Leading Ugandan cement producer with approximately 1.5 million MTPA capacity, serving Uganda and regional markets.

resources.name: Limestone Quarries
resources.detail: Captive limestone deposits at each plant location. The Bamburi quarry rehabilitation into Haller Park is an internationally recognized ecological restoration.

resources.name: Distribution Network
resources.detail: Extensive depot and distribution network covering Kenya, Uganda, and neighbouring East African markets.

projects.impact: Bamburi Cement has supplied the building materials for East Africa's most iconic infrastructure projects over seven decades, from the Nairobi-Mombasa highway to Thika Superhighway and numerous commercial developments. The company directly employs over 2,000 people across Kenya and Uganda. The Bamburi Nature Trail (Haller Park) at the rehabilitated Mombasa quarry is one of the world's most celebrated industrial ecological restoration projects, transforming a barren limestone quarry into a thriving ecosystem that attracts over 100,000 visitors annually.

projects.name: Rosslyn Automotive Hub (Tshwane Automotive SEZ)
projects.tagline: South Africa's automotive nerve centre — where BMW, Nissan, and Ford build for Africa and the world.
projects.overview: The Rosslyn Automotive Hub in Tshwane (Pretoria) is one of Africa's most concentrated automotive manufacturing clusters, anchored by BMW South Africa's Rosslyn plant, Nissan's Rosslyn factory, and supported by the Tshwane Automotive Special Economic Zone (SEZ). BMW has produced vehicles in Rosslyn since 1973 and currently manufactures the 3 Series exclusively for the South African and sub-Saharan African market, along with the X3 for global export. Nissan produces the Navara pickup and NP200 at its adjacent facility. The Tshwane Automotive SEZ, established to attract component manufacturers, provides purpose-built facilities for Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers, strengthening the local automotive value chain and increasing local content.
objectives: Anchor world-class automotive manufacturing in South Africa with BMW and Nissan as flagship producers
Attract automotive component suppliers to the Tshwane Automotive SEZ to increase local content
Produce vehicles for domestic, continental, and global export markets under the APDP II programme
Create and sustain thousands of high-skilled manufacturing jobs in the Tshwane region
Develop the skills pipeline for South Africa's automotive sector through training partnerships
Position Tshwane as a leading African hub for advanced manufacturing and Industry 4.0
technologies: BMW body shop with over 500 robots for automated welding and body assembly
Cathodic dip coating and multi-layer paint processes meeting BMW global quality standards
Just-in-sequence supplier delivery from SEZ-based component manufacturers
Advanced quality measurement systems including 3D coordinate measuring machines
BMW iFACTORY digital manufacturing platform incorporating AI and IoT technologies
Nissan's integrated production system (NPW — Nissan Production Way) for lean manufacturing

milestones.title: BMW Rosslyn Plant Opens
milestones.description: BMW South Africa begins vehicle production at its Rosslyn plant, making it one of BMW's longest-running international manufacturing operations.

milestones.title: Nissan Rosslyn Plant Established
milestones.description: Nissan establishes its South African manufacturing operation adjacent to BMW in Rosslyn, producing the Hardbody and later NP200 and Navara pickup trucks.

milestones.title: Tshwane Automotive SEZ Approved
milestones.description: The Department of Trade, Industry and Competition approves the Tshwane Automotive Special Economic Zone to cluster component suppliers near the BMW and Nissan plants.

milestones.title: BMW X3 Export Production
milestones.description: BMW South Africa begins production of the X3 at Rosslyn for export to global markets, representing a major investment and vote of confidence in South African manufacturing.

milestones.title: SEZ Phase 1 Operational
milestones.description: First Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers commence operations within the Tshwane Automotive SEZ, supplying components directly to BMW and Nissan assembly lines.

milestones.title: APDP II and Future Models
milestones.description: BMW and Nissan continue investing under South Africa's APDP II automotive incentive programme. BMW prepares for next-generation model production at Rosslyn.

resources.name: BMW Rosslyn Plant

resources.name: Nissan Rosslyn Plant

resources.name: Tshwane Automotive SEZ

resources.name: Supplier Park

resources.name: Skilled Automotive Workforce

projects.impact: The Rosslyn Automotive Hub directly employs over 6,000 people at BMW and Nissan plants combined, with thousands more in the supplier cluster and SEZ. BMW alone exports approximately 70% of its Rosslyn production to over 40 countries, generating significant foreign exchange. The Tshwane Automotive SEZ is attracting new investment that deepens the local supply chain and increases the percentage of locally manufactured components. The hub is a cornerstone of South Africa's automotive sector, which contributes approximately 4.9% of GDP and is the country's largest manufacturing exporter.

projects.name: Mara Phones Manufacturing Plant
projects.tagline: Africa's first smartphone factory — made in Rwanda, designed for the continent.
projects.overview: Mara Phones, established by Ashish Thakkar's Mara Group, opened Africa's first smartphone manufacturing plant in Kigali, Rwanda in October 2019. Unlike other African phone assembly operations that import semi-assembled components, Mara manufactures smartphones from the motherboard level, making it the first true smartphone factory on the continent. The plant produces affordable Android-based smartphones (Mara X and Mara Z models) targeting first-time smartphone users in Africa. The facility was opened by President Paul Kagame and represents Rwanda's push to position itself as a technology manufacturing hub. A second Mara Phones factory was also opened in Durban, South Africa.
objectives: Manufacture affordable smartphones from the motherboard level entirely on African soil
Provide first-time smartphone access to millions of Africans at price points under $100
Build local electronics manufacturing skills and a technology workforce in Rwanda
Demonstrate that complex electronics manufacturing is viable in Africa
Support Rwanda's digital transformation and Made in Rwanda agenda
Reduce Africa's dependency on imported phones by building local production capacity
technologies: Surface mount technology (SMT) lines for motherboard assembly with automated pick-and-place machines
Full phone assembly including display, battery, camera module, and housing integration
Automated optical inspection (AOI) and quality testing stations for assembled circuit boards
Android operating system customisation and software loading for Mara-branded devices
ESD-safe (electrostatic discharge) cleanroom manufacturing environment
Packaging and logistics automation for finished product distribution

milestones.title: Mara Phones Announced
milestones.description: Ashish Thakkar's Mara Group announces plans to build Africa's first smartphone manufacturing plant in Kigali, securing investment and government support.

milestones.title: Kigali Factory Opened
milestones.description: President Paul Kagame officially opens the Mara Phones factory in the Kigali Special Economic Zone in October 2019. First Mara X and Mara Z smartphones produced.

milestones.title: Durban Factory Opened
milestones.description: A second Mara Phones factory opens in Durban, South Africa, expanding the company's manufacturing footprint to two African countries.

milestones.title: Initial Production Ramp-Up
milestones.description: Production ramps up with smartphones sold through Rwandan retail channels. The Mara X is priced at approximately $60 targeting first-time smartphone buyers.

milestones.title: Market Challenges and Restructuring
milestones.description: Mara Phones faces competitive pressures from established Chinese brands in the African market. The company explores partnerships and market repositioning.

milestones.title: Continued Operations and New Models
milestones.description: Mara Phones continues operations focusing on affordable connectivity devices and exploring B2B partnerships with governments and educational institutions.

resources.name: SMT Production Lines
resources.detail: Surface mount technology lines with automated component placement machines, reflow soldering ovens, and inspection systems for motherboard manufacturing.

resources.name: Assembly Lines
resources.detail: Manual and semi-automated assembly stations for integrating displays, batteries, cameras, and housings into finished smartphones.

resources.name: Quality Testing Lab
resources.detail: Testing stations for functional testing, RF calibration, camera quality, battery performance, and drop testing of finished devices.

resources.name: Component Supply Chain
resources.detail: Components sourced from global suppliers including Qualcomm (chipsets), display manufacturers, and battery cell producers.

resources.name: Trained Technicians
resources.detail: Rwandan technicians trained in electronics manufacturing, SMT operation, and quality control.

projects.impact: Mara Phones demonstrated that smartphone manufacturing from the motherboard level is achievable in Africa, challenging the assumption that such manufacturing is only viable in Asia. The Kigali factory provided skilled electronics manufacturing jobs for hundreds of Rwandans and built a foundation of technical capability. While facing significant competitive challenges from established global brands, Mara Phones symbolises Africa's manufacturing ambition and has inspired discussions about electronics manufacturing policy across the continent. The project supports Rwanda's Vision 2050 goal of building a technology-enabled economy.
